entry.title: Re: Migrate Invoice Summary Data
entry.content: Would you please give more details as to what summary invoice information you would like to migrate, and for how many years of data. Previous vendor invoices might have been paid now, we would like to know what details that you like to migrate- Vendor code, invoice number etc. What is the purpose of migration.

entry.title: Re: Week 1 : Set up the Basic Trial account enabled with Joule for Developers
French e-invoicing & S/4HANA Cloud: partial vs residual payments for e-reporting?
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

As part of a project on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, we are preparing for the upcoming French e-invoicing and e-reporting reform .

From what I understand, the regulation does not define a “partial payment” status, but instead requires reporting of payment events (date and amount).

In this context, I have a few questions:

  • Is there a preferred approach in SAP (partial payment vs residual item) to ensure alignment between AR open items and reported payment events?
  • How are partial payments typically handled for e-reporting (multiple payment events for one invoice)?
  • Has SAP provided any official guidance or best practices on this topic?

The goal is to stay fully compliant while keeping a clean-core approach in Public Cloud.

Any feedback or experience would be highly appreciated!

Thanks,
Corinne

entry.title: Re: How to deploy multiple intents of same Fiori app to W...
entry.content:
Hi Brendon,

Good day ! thank you for the response.

Well that's exactly our issue was and raised a case with sap.

Our scenario is:

  • App imported from WebIde to BAS for NEO to CF migration, in manifest crossApplication Inbound we had 2 intents (1 with a tile & other no tile "hidelauncher":true), because for some users we need to be a tile, for some users based on role they can only do cross app navigation into detail page. This is how it was configured in NEO.
  • App deployed to cloud foundry.
  • In WorkZone content explorer we see only 1 app.

We followed sap help doc: https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-portal-service/sap-cloud-portal-service-on-cloud-foundry/configuring-multiple-intents-in-app-navigation?locale=en-US

Since we don't see 2 apps in workzone we raised a case with SAP and they responded stating "After checking the issue internally, we found that due to certain restrictions in Work Zone, it is not possible to have multiple intents for the same Fiori application in Work Zone." and this KBA0003585268.

Well if this multiple intents is working for you. Can you help us detailing how you achieved it?

Thank you, Sai

entry.title: Business Partner @ SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public & Private Edition
entry.content:
Welcome to the starting point of a new blog series!

In this series, I’d like to take you along on my journey through my very first Public Cloud project. Just like in my previous posts, the focus will once again be on one of my favorite topics: business partners.
Working in the Public Cloud comes with a few special characteristics – some charming, some challenging. And that’s exactly what I want to share with you here.
My experiences are based on releases 2508 and 2602. Especially in the Public Cloud world, things move quickly. By the time you read this, certain features may already look slightly different, new capabilities may have appeared, or old ones may have been refined. I’ll do my best to keep the content as fresh and up to date as possible.
If you notice something important that’s missing or spot a detail that’s outdated or incorrect, feel free to drop a comment. That way, we can consolidate our knowledge and help each other grow.
Let’s begin with the product name, which has gone through (and will go through) a bit of evolution:

until 2026: SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition or SAP S/4HANA PEC
from 2026 onward: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (tbc 😉)

A key companion throughout any SAP Cloud journey is SAP Activate. I’ll be using this framework as a reference in this series as well.

SAP Activate in a nutshell:
SAP Activate is a cloud adoption framework that combines solution‑specific best practices, expert guidance, and a clear, phase‑driven approach to simplify and structure your transformation journey.

Official SAP Activate Page SAP Activate | Accelerate SAP Cloud ERP Deployment

Access SAP Activate via Road Map Viewer  Road Map Viewer - SAP for Me

At this point I would like to sort my subsequent linked blogs to corresponding SAP Activate Phase

 

 Solution Scope and Adoption PlanningFit-to-Standard WorkshopsSolution Configuration  
  Fit-to-Standard Workshops Documentation and DesignTest Preparation - Test Scripts for CALM  
  Data Load Preparation   

 

To speak in SAP Activate language, you see under each phase a deliverable. My plan is to create a blog for each of this deliverable and link it here.
Subscribe this blog to keep updated if a new blog is created and linked.

entry.title: SAP Translation Hub Rolls Out New Languages
entry.content:
Following our recent expansion of SAP Translation Hub’s LLM-based translation capabilities, we’re excited to announce the addition of seven new languages to our offering:

  • Armenian
  • Bengali
  • Georgian
  • Khmer
  • Lao
  • Maori
  • Uzbek

These languages are now available through our LLM-based translation provider in both Document Translation and Software Translation on SAP BTP (regions EU10, EU11, and US10).

This expansion reflects our ongoing commitment to supporting businesses operating in diverse linguistic markets. Whether you’re localizing custom applications for users in Southeast Asia, the Caucasus, or the Pacific, SAP Translation Hub now offers even broader coverage to translate your business.

 Use the APIs or simply select LLM as your translation engine in the Document Translation or Software Translation interface, and you’ll find these new languages available in your source and target language options.

As always, we recommend reviewing LLM-generated translations before deployment to ensure they meet your quality standards.

Learn More

  • SAP Translation Hub Documentation
  • LLM-based Translation Supported Languages
  • Contact us: translationhub@sap.com

Have you been waiting for support for a specific language? Let us know in the comments which languages would help your localization projects!

SAP Translation Hub

entry.title: How to Achieve Storage Location-Level Planning in SAP Cloud ERP - Step-by-Step Guide
entry.content:
Introduction

Material Requirements Planning (MRP) plays a critical role in ensuring material availability while optimizing inventory levels. In traditional SAP systems, businesses often leveraged storage location-level MRP for granular planning. However, in SAP Cloud ERP, storage location-level MRP is not directly supported.

To address this limitation, organizations can use MRP Areas to achieve similar functionality. This blog explains how MRP Areas—specifically storage location MRP Areas—can be used as an effective alternative.

Things to Know

What is MRP?

MRP (Material Requirements Planning) is a planning tool used to ensure that the right materials are available at the right time and in the right quantity. It considers demand, current stock, and procurement proposals.

Plant-Level MRP

At the plant level, MRP plans materials considering the entire plant stock. It does not differentiate between storage locations, which may lead to inefficiencies when materials are managed differently across locations.

Storage Location-Level MRP

In legacy SAP systems, this allowed planning at a more granular level, enabling businesses to plan materials separately for each storage location.
In SAP Cloud ERP, this functionality is not directly available.

What is an MRP Area?

MRP Area is an organizational unit that allows MRP to be executed for:

  • A whole plant
  • A specific storage location
  • A subcontractor

By defining storage locations as MRP Areas, you can simulate storage location-level planning.

Business Scenario

Consider a manufacturing company operating on SAP Cloud ERP, Public Cloud, where the same raw material is stored in two different locations within a single plant—one close to the production line and the other in a central warehouse. For instance, bolts are kept in a production storage location for daily shop floor consumption and in a warehouse storage location for bulk storage and backup supply. The production area requires frequent replenishment in smaller quantities, whereas the warehouse manages larger volumes with less frequent planning cycles.

When MRP is executed at the plant level, the system aggregates stock from both locations and plans collectively. This can result in misleading availability, where overall stock appears sufficient, but the production location may still experience shortages.

To address this, the company aims to plan material requirements separately for each storage location—ensuring uninterrupted supply to production while maintaining optimal inventory in the warehouse.

This scenario can be effectively handled in SAP Cloud ERP, Public Cloud by creating MRP Areas of type 02 (storage location) for each location, enabling independent planning and better inventory control.

Now, Let’s deep dive into how to set up storage location MRP Area and how it impacts the MRP run.

Configuration Steps

Define MRP Areas  under the configuration customization node Material Requirement Planning (MRP) > Define MRP Area for Plant/Storage Locations

Add a new entry to create MRP Area (ZSLOCWP) for the storage location 1 (here it is SLoc – 118S) and make sure the MRP Area Type is 02

In the details screen, Enter the MRP Area description, Receiving Storage location (If more than one storage locations assigned to the MRP area, then the receiving storage location will be the one to which all the components are received), enable the checkbox ‘Assign Materials automatically’ and fill in the MRP Area Profile as SMRPTYPE_PD (if the specified storage location needs to plan separately) or SMRPTYPE_ND (if the specified storage location needs to be excluded from planning). In this blog we are considering the former scenario (i.e. to plan the specified storage location separately)

Under the tab ‘Assign storage locations,’ add storage locations that are needs to be planned separately from the Plant level MRP

Navigate to the Fiori app ‘Change material master’ (tcode: MM02) > under MRP1 View > Under the section MRP Area, click the button ‘MRP Areas’ > Add the newly created MRP Area ‘ZSLOCWP’ for storage location 118S and make sure that material is extended to the same storage location.

In the details of MRP Area, enter the MRP Type, MRP Controller (which acts as a filter while scheduling MRP Run only specific to that MRP Area), Lot Size Procedure and then click on Adopt

Similar to the above steps creating MRP Area ZSLOCWP1 for another storage location (let’s say 118F) in the customization configuration screen.

In the details screen, provide the description, receiving storage location and MRP Area Profile as SMRPTYPE_PD to plan separately for the specified location. If more than one storage locations assigned to the MRP area, then the receiving storage location will be the one to which all the components are received.

Here assigns the other storage location 118F that needs to be planned separately from other locations/plant.

Navigate to MM02 > Add the secondly created MRP Area ‘ZSLOCWP1’ and make sure that the material is extended to the storage location for which the MRP area is created.

Fill in the mandatory fields for MRP Area 

Production Planning Steps

Goto MD61 (Create Planned Independent Requirement) > maintain the requirement quantity for the material at MRP area level and make sure that version is active to considered it for MRP run. Here, maintained 100EA for the month of March 2026 and April 2026 for both the storage location level MRP Area and Plant MRP Area.

Navigate to the Fiori app ‘Monitor Material Coverage Net/Individual segments’ > Filter with the material and plant number > select the displayed line item and click on Manage Materials option at the top of the list. It displays the IndReq created ate MRP Area level .

Goto Fiori app ‘Schedule MRP Runs’ > fill in the required parameters which includes, Plant, Material, MRP Controller (if required). Though we don’t have separate field for MRP Area, MRP live considers all the MRP Areas defined in the plant for the specified material and performs planning at each MRP Area level. Once all the data are entered , click on ‘Schedule’. Job will be scheduled at the backend.

Once the job got finished, Job log can be viewed by clicking on the files icon in the Results column.

In the below screen, it is visible that the MRP Live considered all the MRP Area for planning and planned each of them separately. Hence created separated line item for each of the MRP Areas.

For the MRP Area ZSLOCWP (storage location 118S), it created 2 Planned orders for the current month with quantity 85EA (as there already exists some stock in the location 118S) and the other with 100EA for the next month.

For the MRP Area ZSLOCWP1 (storage location 118F), it created 2 Planned orders for both the months each with quantity 100EA (as there no stock exists in the location 118F). It considered only that storage location stock and not the existing stock in the plant.

For the Plant MRP Area 1811, it created 2 Planned orders for both the months with quantity 100EA and it didn’t considered the stock in the storage location MRP Area.

Conclusion

Although storage location-level MRP is not directly supported in SAP Cloud ERP, MRP Areas provide a robust and flexible alternative. By leveraging storage location MRP Areas, businesses can achieve granular planning, better inventory control, and improved operational efficiency.

Hope this blog helps you to better understand how to effectively use MRP Areas to achieve storage location-level planning in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud. If you have any questions, feedback, or would like to share how you are implementing this in your organization, feel free to drop your comments below.

Happy Learning!! 🙂

entry.title: ERPsim LAC Competition 2026, organized by ERPsim Lab - HEC Montréal
entry.content:
🌎 Registration Link: ERPsim LAC Competition 2026 Tickets, Friday, May 15 from 4 pm to 7 pm CEST | Eventbrite

 For all ERPsim competitions visit: https://erpsim.hec.ca/en/competition
 

Overview - ERPsim LAC Competition - All you need to know

The road towards the International ERPsim Final!

Get ready to take part in this exhilarating regional ERPsim competition and aim for the sought-after top three positions. The three winning teams of each regional ERPsim competition get awarded with the unique opportunity to participate in this contest where the most exceptional teams from around the world will converge to compete for the prestigious title.

The Game

Teams will play the ERPsim Manufacturing Sustainability Game. Students will apply their business knowledge to make strategic decisions about their virtual company and input their plans to SAP S/4HANA using SAP Fiori. The ERPsim platform will simulate business activity based on these decisions. The virtual company with the highest company valuation at the end of the 4-round game is the winner.​

Who can register?

Your university must be member of SAP University Alliances at the time of the competition and be located in the LAC region to participate.

University-level eligibility for ERPsim academic competitions is contingent upon the integration of both the ERPsim game and Business Builders into the institution's teaching curriculum. Participation is granted to the university provided that at least one meaningful pedagogical activity—whether in-class or extracurricular—utilizes both platforms.

Teams are encouraged to have experience with SAP S/4 HANA and ERPsim prior to event - no training is provided on the day of the event. An information and practice session will be offered one week prior to the event.

Each university may register one team of 3 to 5 students and one or two faculty coaches. The main coach is responsible to register the team. We will communicate with the team through the main coach. Diverse teams are encouraged.

Rules

  • Participation is FREE. Registration will close when team registrations are at capacity.
  • The event is offered in a virtual format.
  • The coach is the main point of contact for all event logistics and communications.
  • Students names and university email addresses are provided prior to the event.
  • The teams will play 4 rounds of the ERPsim Manufacturing Sustainability Game. Each round will be of 20 virtual days of 1 minute each.
  • Teams will be ranked based on their company valuation.
  • No cheating is allowed! All teams must always behave ethically. The organizers reserve the rights to disqualify teams who are not playing fairly.

Prizes

  • Digital badges for the top 3 teams, the coaches, and the registered participants.
  • The top 3 teams qualify for the International ERPsim Final.

entry.title: How to get body for InA Service Request without cloud tools
entry.content:
Hello, I am exploring possibilities of the new CDS Projection Views, specifically the Analytical Query, but the important thing to mention is that I'm on prem.
And as the Analytical Projection Views can only be accessed via InA Services, I cannot really make the GetResponse request to work and return data (The main issue is the Parameters (Variables in InA Request) that I need to pass)

So I would like to know if there is a way to get the request for this specific Analytical Query ON PREM.

entry.title: RAP: Factory Action to copy Billing Header and its Item(s) using Create by association
entry.content:
In this blog, I am going to explain how we can use create by association with factory action in SAP RAP environment for complex use case like copying duplicate billing header with its associated child entities.

Let's understand what action in RAP is, and what is factory action first.

If you want to perform any operation other than those four basic operations (CRUD), we can use action keyword in RAP. It helps us to do custom action like approve billing doc or change any field value like status.

We have three types of actions in RAP:

  1. Instance Action: we need to select a record to perform action.
  2. Static Actions: that are not tied to a specific instance.
  3. Factory Actions: used for creating the copy of already existing instance. We will see this more in details.

Create by Association: In scenario where we cannot create child entity as it is depend on header, we need to use create by association. Used to create deep entity.

So, let's start with the steps to generate our basic application. Please have a look.

1. My header table look like this: 👇

@EndUserText.label : 'Billing Header'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table ztb_billheader {

  key client         : abap.clnt not null;
  key bill_id        : abap.numc(10) not null;
  bill_type          : abap.char(4);
  bill_date          : abap.dats;
  customer_id        : kunnr;
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'ztb_billheader.currency'
  net_amount         : abap.curr(15,2);
  currency           : waers;
  sales_org          : vkorg;
  createdby          : syuname;
  createdat          : timestamp;
  lastchangedby      : syuname;
  lastchangedat      : timestamp;
  locallastchangedat : timestamp;
  status             : abap.char(1);
  statustext         : abap.char(10);

}

I am not using managed numbering here as I want custom Bill Id.

2. My Item table look like this: 👇

@EndUserText.label : 'Billing Item'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table ztb_billitem {

  key client         : abap.clnt not null;
  key bill_id        : abap.char(10) not null;
  key item_no        : abap.numc(6) not null;
  material_id        : matnr;
  description        : abap.char(40);
  @Semantics.quantity.unitOfMeasure : 'ztb_billitem.uom'
  quantity           : abap.quan(13,3);
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'ztb_billitem.currency'
  item_amount        : abap.curr(15,2);
  currency           : waers;
  uom                : meins;
  createdby          : syuname;
  createdat          : timestamp;
  lastchangedby      : syuname;
  lastchangedat      : timestamp;
  locallastchangedat : timestamp;

}

3. I have created interface view on top of header table: You can skip that also. It looks like this: 👇

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Interface View for Bill header'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define view entity ZI_BillHeader as select from ztb_billheader
{
    key bill_id as BillId,
    bill_type as BillType,
    bill_date as BillDate,
    customer_id as CustomerId,
    @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'Currency'
    net_amount as NetAmount,
    currency as Currency,
    sales_org as SalesOrg,
    createdby as Createdby,
    createdat as Createdat,
    lastchangedby as Lastchangedby,
    lastchangedat as Lastchangedat,
    locallastchangedat as Locallastchangedat,
    status as status,
    statustext as StatusText
}

4. I have created root view on top of header interface view: 👇

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Root View Bill Header'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define root view entity ZR_BillHeader as select from ZI_BillHeader
composition[0..*] of ZI_BillItem as _Item
{
    key BillId,
    BillType,
    BillDate,
    CustomerId,
    @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'Currency'
    NetAmount,
    Currency,
    SalesOrg,
    Createdby,
    Createdat,
    Lastchangedby,
    Lastchangedat,
    Locallastchangedat,
    status,
    StatusText,
    _Item // Make association public
}

5. I have created interface view on top of item table: 👇

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Interface View for Bill Item'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true

define view entity ZI_BillItem 
as select from ztb_billitem
association to parent ZR_BillHeader as _header on $projection.BillId = _header.BillId
{
    key bill_id as BillId,
    key item_no as ItemNo,
    material_id as MaterialId,
    description as Description,
    @Semantics.quantity.unitOfMeasure: 'Uom'
    quantity as Quantity,
    @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'Currency'
    item_amount as ItemAmount,
    currency as Currency,
    uom as Uom,
    createdby as Createdby,
    createdat as Createdat,
    lastchangedby as Lastchangedby,
    lastchangedat as Lastchangedat,
    locallastchangedat as Locallastchangedat,
    _header
}

6. Created projection view on top of root view: 👇

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Projection View Bill Header'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true
define root view entity ZP_BillHeader
  provider contract transactional_query
  as projection on ZR_BillHeader
{
  key BillId,
      BillType,
      BillDate,
      CustomerId,
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'Currency'
      NetAmount,
      Currency,
      SalesOrg,
      Createdby,
      Createdat,
      Lastchangedby,
      Lastchangedat,
      Locallastchangedat,
      @ObjectModel.text.element: ['StatusText']
      status,
      StatusText,
      /* Associations */
      _Item : redirected to composition child ZP_BillItem
}

7. Similarly created projection view for item entity: 👇

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Projection View Bill Item'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true
define view entity ZP_BillItem
  as projection on ZI_BillItem
{
  key BillId,
  key ItemNo,
      MaterialId,
      Description,
      @Semantics.quantity.unitOfMeasure: 'Uom'
      Quantity,
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'Currency'
      ItemAmount,
      Currency,
      Uom,
      Createdby,
      Createdat,
      Lastchangedby,
      Lastchangedat,
      Locallastchangedat,
      /* Associations */
      _header : redirected to parent ZP_BillHeader
}

8. My metadata looks like this: 👇 (Please make sure I haven't given labels for each field, so in UI for few field it will missing. My core agenda is to elaborate factory action) 

Header: 

@Metadata.layer: #CORE
@UI.headerInfo:{
typeName: 'Billing Document',
typeNamePlural: 'Billing Documents',
title: { type: #STANDARD, label: 'Biiling Document', value: 'BillId' }}
annotate entity ZP_BillHeader with
{
  .facet: [{ //id: 'BillDocHeader',
  label: 'Billing header',
  position: 10,
  purpose: #STANDARD,
  type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE
   },
   { //id: 'BillDocItem',
   label: 'Bill Item',
   position: 20,
   purpose: #STANDARD,
   type: #LINEITEM_REFERENCE,
   targetElement: '_Item'
   }]

  .lineItem: [{ position:10 },
   { position: 10,
   type: #FOR_ACTION,
  dataAction: 'ApproveBill',
  label: 'Approve Bill' },
     { position: 20,
   type: #FOR_ACTION,
  dataAction: 'CopyBilldoc',
  label: 'Copy Bill' }]
  .selectionField: [{position: 1  }]
  .identification: [{ position: 1  }]
  BillId;
  .lineItem: [{ position:20 }]
  .identification: [{ position: 2  }]
  BillType;
  .lineItem: [{ position:30 }]
  BillDate;
  .lineItem: [{ position:40 }]
  .identification: [{ position: 3  }]
  CustomerId;
  .lineItem: [{ position:50 }]
  .identification: [{ position: 4  }]
  NetAmount;
  .lineItem: [{ position:60 }]
  .identification: [{ position: 5 }]
  Currency;
  .lineItem: [{ position:70 }]
  .identification: [{ position: 6  }]
  SalesOrg;
  .lineItem: [{ position:80 }]
  .identification: [{ position: 7  }]
  .textArrangement: #TEXT_ONLY
  status;

}

Item: 

@Metadata.layer: #CORE
annotate entity ZP_BillItem with
{
  .facet: [ {
    id:       'BillDocItem',
    purpose:  #STANDARD,
    type:     #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE,
    label:    'Billing Doc Item',
    position: 10
  }]
  .lineItem: [{ position:10 }]
  .identification: [{ position: 1 }]
  BillId;
  .lineItem: [{ position:20 }]
  .identification: [{ position: 1 }]
  ItemNo;
  .lineItem: [{ position:30 }]
  .identification: [{ position: 1 }]
  MaterialId;
  .lineItem: [{ position:40 }]
  Description;
  .lineItem: [{ position:50 }]
  Quantity;
  .lineItem: [{ position:70 }]
  ItemAmount;
  .lineItem: [{ position:80 }]
  Currency;
  .lineItem: [{ position:60 }]
  Uom;
  .lineItem: [{ position:90 }]
  Createdby;
  .lineItem: [{ position:100 }]
  Createdat;
  .lineItem: [{ position:110 }]

  /* Associations */
  _header;

}

Now we have basic structure ready !!! Main steps for our topic start from here.

9. Create behavior definition on top of root entity. Just right click on the root view entity and select new behavior definition as shown below 👇

Once behavior definition is generated, use quick fix to implement behavior class. 

10. Now add below syntax in behavior definition:

  factory action CopyBilldoc [1];

The whole behavior definition looks like this: 

managed implementation in class zbp_r_billheader unique;
strict ( 2 );

with draft;
define behavior for ZR_BillHeader //alias <alias_name>
persistent table ztb_billheader
draft table ztb_billhdr_drf
lock master total etag Lastchangedat
authorization master ( instance, global )
etag master Locallastchangedat
{
  create;
  update;
  delete;
  draft action Edit;
  draft action Resume;
  draft determine action Prepare;
  draft action Activate optimized;
  draft action Discard;
  field ( readonly : update, mandatory : create ) BillId;


  action ( features : instance ) ApproveBill result [1] $self;
  factory action CopyBilldoc [1];
  side effects
  {
    action CopyBilldoc affects $self;
  }
  association _Item { create; with draft; }

  mapping for ztb_billheader
    {
      BillId             = bill_id;
      BillType           = bill_type;
      BillDate           = bill_date;
      CustomerId         = customer_id;
      NetAmount          = net_amount;
      Currency           = currency;
      SalesOrg           = sales_org;
      CreatedBy          = createdby;
      CreateDat          = createdat;
      LastChangedBy      = lastchangedby;
      LastChangeDat      = lastchangedat;
      LocalLastChangeDat = locallastchangedat;
      status             = status;
      StatusText         = statustext;
    }
}

define behavior for ZI_BillItem //alias <alias_name>
persistent table ztb_billitem
draft table ztb_billitem_drf
lock dependent by _header
authorization dependent by _header
//etag master <field_name>
{
  update;
  delete;
  field ( readonly : update, mandatory : create ) BillId, ItemNo;
  association _header { with draft; }

  mapping for ztb_billitem
    {
      BillId             = bill_id;
      ItemNo             = item_no;
      MaterialId         = material_id;
      Description        = description;
      Quantity           = quantity;
      ItemAmount         = item_amount;
      Currency           = currency;
      Uom                = uom;
      CreatedBy          = createdby;
      CreateDat          = createdat;
      LastChangedBy      = lastchangedby;
      LastChangeDat      = lastchangedat;
      LocalLastChangeDat = locallastchangedat;
    }
}

Here, I have added extra things like draft, custom action and side effects. But for now, just focus on the line number 24.

11. Activate and use quick fix to generate this method in behavior class. 

12. Use below code inside the generated factory action method. 👇

METHOD CopyBilldoc.

    " Declaration of internal tables.

    DATA: lt_bill     TYPE TABLE FOR CREATE ZR_BillHeader\\ZR_BillHeader,
          lt_billitem TYPE TABLE FOR CREATE ZR_BillHeader\_item.

    DATA: lv_billId TYPE c LENGTH 10.
   " Reading highest existing bill id and incrementing it by 1 to use as new bill id
    SELECT BillId FROM ZR_BillHeader ORDER BY BillId DESCENDING
        INTO _billid UP TO 1 ROWS.
    ENDSELECT.

    lv_billId = lv_billId + 1.

   " Read by association 

    READ ENTITIES OF ZR_BillHeader  IN LOCAL MODE
    ENTITY ZR_BillHeader
    ALL FIELDS WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
    RESULT DATA(lt_data1)
    ENTITY ZR_BillHeader BY \_item
    ALL FIELDS WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
    RESULT DATA(lt_data2)
    FAILED failed.
   
   " Appending to create a new structure
    LOOP AT lt_data1 ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<ls_data1>).

      APPEND VALUE #( %cid = keys[ KEY entity %key = <ls_data1>-%key ]-%cid

                      "%is_draft = keys[ KEY entity %key = <ls_data1>-%key ]-%param-%is_draft

                      %data = CORRESPONDING #( <ls_data1> EXCEPT BillId )

                      ) TO lt_bill ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<new_bill_doc>).

      <new_bill_doc>-BillId = CONV #( lv_billId ).
      <new_bill_doc>-BillDate = cl_abap_context_info=>get_system_date(  ).

      LOOP AT lt_data2 INTO DATA(ls_data2).
        APPEND VALUE #(
        %cid_ref = <new_bill_doc>-%cid
        "BillId = <new_bill_doc>-BillId
        %target = VALUE #( ( %cid = <new_bill_doc>-%cid
        BillId = <new_bill_doc>-BillId
        MaterialId = ls_data2-MaterialId
        ItemNo = ls_data2-ItemNo ) )
         ) TO lt_billitem ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<new_bill_item>).

      ENDLOOP.

    ENDLOOP.

    "Create BO instance by copy.

    MODIFY ENTITIES OF ZR_BillHeader IN LOCAL MODE
    ENTITY ZR_BillHeader
    CREATE FIELDS ( BillId BillDate BillType Currency CustomerId NetAmount SalesOrg )
    WITH VALUE #( (
    %cid = <new_bill_doc>-%cid
    BillId = <new_bill_doc>-BillId
    BillDate = <new_bill_doc>-BillDate
    Currency = <new_bill_doc>-Currency
    CustomerId = <new_bill_doc>-CustomerId
    NetAmount = <new_bill_doc>-NetAmount
    SalesOrg = <new_bill_doc>-SalesOrg ) )
    CREATE BY \_item FIELDS ( ItemNo MaterialId )
    WITH VALUE #( (
    %cid_ref = <new_bill_doc>-%cid
    %target = VALUE #( FOR i IN lt_billitem
        FOR j IN i-%target (
    %cid = j-ItemNo
    ItemNo = j-ItemNo
    MaterialId = j-MaterialId
) ) ) )
    MAPPED DATA(mapped_create).

    " Set values to front end.

    mapped-ZR_BillHeader = mapped_create-ZR_BillHeader.


  ENDMETHOD.

The whole my behavior class looks like this: 👇

CLASS lhc_ZR_BillHeader DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_handler.
  PRIVATE SECTION.

    METHODS get_instance_authorizations FOR INSTANCE AUTHORIZATION
      IMPORTING keys REQUEST requested_authorizations FOR ZR_BillHeader RESULT result.

    METHODS get_global_authorizations FOR GLOBAL AUTHORIZATION
      IMPORTING REQUEST requested_authorizations FOR ZR_BillHeader RESULT result.
    METHODS ApproveBill FOR MODIFY
      IMPORTING keys FOR ACTION ZR_BillHeader~ApproveBill RESULT result.
    METHODS get_instance_features FOR INSTANCE FEATURES
      IMPORTING keys REQUEST requested_features FOR ZR_BillHeader RESULT result.
    METHODS copybilldoc FOR MODIFY
      IMPORTING keys FOR ACTION zr_billheader~copybilldoc.

ENDCLASS.

CLASS lhc_ZR_BillHeader IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD get_instance_authorizations.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD get_global_authorizations.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD ApproveBill.

*    DATA: lt_data TYPE TABLE FOR UPDATE ZR_BillHeader.
*    DATA: lt_result TYPE TABLE FOR READ RESULT ZR_BillHeader.
*
*    " Read current data
*    READ ENTITIES OF zr_billheader IN LOCAL MODE
*      ENTITY ZR_BillHeader
*      ALL FIELDS
*      WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
*      RESULT DATA(lt_billheader).
*
*    LOOP AT lt_billheader INTO DATA(ls_order).
*      APPEND VALUE #(
*      %tky = ls_order-%tky
*      status = 'A' ) TO lt_data.
*    ENDLOOP.
*    " Prepare update
*    MODIFY ENTITIES OF ZR_BillHeader IN LOCAL MODE
*      ENTITY ZR_BillHeader
*      UPDATE FIELDS ( status )
*      WITH lt_data.
*
*
*    READ ENTITIES OF ZR_BillHeader IN LOCAL MODE
*    ENTITY ZR_BillHeader
*    ALL FIELDS WITH
*    CORRESPONDING #( keys )
*    RESULT lt_result.
*
*    result = VALUE #(
*    FOR ls IN lt_result
*    ( %tky = ls-%tky
*    %param = ls ) ).

    MODIFY ENTITIES OF ZR_BillHeader IN LOCAL MODE
               ENTITY ZR_BillHeader
                  UPDATE FROM VALUE #( FOR key IN keys
                  ( %tky = key-%tky
                    status = 'A' " Approved
                    StatusText = 'Approved'
                    %control-status = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
                    %control-StatusText = if_abap_behv=>mk-on ) )
                  FAILED failed
                  REPORTED reported.

    "Read changed data for action result
    READ ENTITIES OF ZR_BillHeader IN LOCAL MODE
      ENTITY ZR_BillHeader
      ALL FIELDS WITH
      CORRESPONDING #( keys )
      RESULT DATA(billheaders).

    result = VALUE #( FOR billheader IN billheaders
             ( %tky   = billheader-%tky
               %param = billheader ) ).

  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD get_instance_features.
    READ ENTITIES OF ZR_BillHeader IN LOCAL MODE
  ENTITY ZR_BillHeader
     FIELDS (  BillId status )
     WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
   RESULT DATA(BillHeaders)
   FAILED failed.

    result =
      VALUE #( FOR BillHeader IN BillHeaders
        ( %key = BillHeader-%key
          %features-%action-ApproveBill = COND #( WHEN BillHeader-status = 'A'
                                                        THEN if_abap_behv=>fc-o-disabled
                                                        ELSE if_abap_behv=>fc-o-enabled )
         ) ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD CopyBilldoc.

    " Copy records assigned to internal table.

    DATA: lt_bill     TYPE TABLE FOR CREATE ZR_BillHeader\\ZR_BillHeader,
          lt_billitem TYPE TABLE FOR CREATE ZR_BillHeader\_item.

    DATA: lv_billId TYPE c LENGTH 10.

    SELECT BillId FROM ZR_BillHeader ORDER BY BillId DESCENDING
        INTO _billid UP TO 1 ROWS.
    ENDSELECT.

    lv_billId = lv_billId + 1.


    READ ENTITIES OF ZR_BillHeader  IN LOCAL MODE
    ENTITY ZR_BillHeader
    ALL FIELDS WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
    RESULT DATA(lt_data1)
    ENTITY ZR_BillHeader BY \_item
    ALL FIELDS WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
    RESULT DATA(lt_data2)
    FAILED failed.

    LOOP AT lt_data1 ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<ls_data1>).

      APPEND VALUE #( %cid = keys[ KEY entity %key = <ls_data1>-%key ]-%cid

                      "%is_draft = keys[ KEY entity %key = <ls_data1>-%key ]-%param-%is_draft

                      %data = CORRESPONDING #( <ls_data1> EXCEPT BillId )

                      ) TO lt_bill ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<new_bill_doc>).

      <new_bill_doc>-BillId = CONV #( lv_billId ).
      <new_bill_doc>-BillDate = cl_abap_context_info=>get_system_date(  ).

      LOOP AT lt_data2 INTO DATA(ls_data2).
        APPEND VALUE #(
        %cid_ref = <new_bill_doc>-%cid
        "BillId = <new_bill_doc>-BillId
        %target = VALUE #( ( %cid = <new_bill_doc>-%cid
        BillId = <new_bill_doc>-BillId
        MaterialId = ls_data2-MaterialId
        ItemNo = ls_data2-ItemNo ) )
         ) TO lt_billitem ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<new_bill_item>).

      ENDLOOP.

    ENDLOOP.

    "Create BO instance by copy.

    MODIFY ENTITIES OF ZR_BillHeader IN LOCAL MODE
    ENTITY ZR_BillHeader
    CREATE FIELDS ( BillId BillDate BillType Currency CustomerId NetAmount SalesOrg )
    WITH VALUE #( (
    %cid = <new_bill_doc>-%cid
    BillId = <new_bill_doc>-BillId
    BillDate = <new_bill_doc>-BillDate
    Currency = <new_bill_doc>-Currency
    CustomerId = <new_bill_doc>-CustomerId
    NetAmount = <new_bill_doc>-NetAmount
    SalesOrg = <new_bill_doc>-SalesOrg ) )
    CREATE BY \_item FIELDS ( ItemNo MaterialId )
    WITH VALUE #( (
    %cid_ref = <new_bill_doc>-%cid
    %target = VALUE #( FOR i IN lt_billitem
        FOR j IN i-%target (
    %cid = j-ItemNo
    ItemNo = j-ItemNo
    MaterialId = j-MaterialId
) ) ) )
    MAPPED DATA(mapped_create).

    " Set values to front end.

    mapped-ZR_BillHeader = mapped_create-ZR_BillHeader.


  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

13. Create behavior projection on top of root projection view. It will look like this 👇

projection;
strict ( 2 );

use draft;
define behavior for ZP_BillHeader //alias <alias_name>
{
  use create;
  use update;
  use delete;
  use action Edit;
  use action Activate;
  use action Discard;
  use action Resume;
  use action Prepare;
  use action ApproveBill;
  use action CopyBilldoc;
  use association _Item { create; with draft;}
}

define behavior for ZP_BillItem //alias <alias_name>
{
  use update;
  use delete;

  use association _header { with draft; }
}

14. Now create the service definition and expose both header and item projection: 

@EndUserText.label: 'Service Definition for Billing Doc'
define service ZSD_BillingDocument {
  expose ZP_BillHeader as BillingHeader;
  expose ZP_BillItem   as BillingItem;
}

15. On top of service definition, create a service binding. I have use Odata V4 as I am using draft functionality. 

16. Finally, it's a result time. Have a first look of our application. 👇

17. Now select any record and click on copyBill. I am selecting second record. 

18. Once I clicked on copyBill, We can see a new BillId is generated with same line items as of source record.

Conclusion: So, we have come to the end of our blog. We have seen how we can implement an use case related to factory action. I want to summarise important steps again required to implement factory action: 

  1. Declare factory action in behavior definition and activate it.
  2. Use quick fix to generate the method in implementation class. 
  3. Use Read by association to read both header and child entity.
  4. Append the new structure.
  5. Create new duplicate BO entry by using create by association.

Thank You for reading my blog. Do comment if you have any doubt or if you know any better way to do this. Good Bye 👋

entry.title: ERPsim EMEA Competition 2026, organized by ERPsim Lab - HEC Montréal
entry.content:
🌍 Registration link: ERPsim EMEA Competition 2026 Tickets, Friday, May 22 from 2 pm to 5 pm CEST | Eventbrite

For all ERPsim competitions visit: https://erpsim.hec.ca/en/competition

Overview ERPsim EMEA Competition - All you need to know

The road towards the International ERPsim Final!

Get ready to take part in this exhilarating regional ERPsim competition and aim for the sought-after top three positions. The three winning teams of each regional ERPsim competition get awarded with the unique opportunity to participate in this contest where the most exceptional teams from around the world will converge to compete for the prestigious title.

The Game

Teams will play the ERPsim Manufacturing Sustainability Game. Students will apply their business knowledge to make strategic decisions about their virtual company and input their plans to SAP S/4HANA using SAP Fiori. The ERPsim platform will simulate business activity based on these decisions. The virtual company with the highest company valuation at the end of the 4-round game is the winner.​

Who can register?

Your university must be member of SAP University Alliances at the time of the competition and be located in the EMEA region to participate.

University-level eligibility for ERPsim academic competitions is contingent upon the integration of both the ERPsim game and Business Builders into the institution's teaching curriculum. Participation is granted to the university provided that at least one meaningful pedagogical activity—whether in-class or extracurricular—utilizes both platforms.

Teams are encouraged to have experience with SAP S/4 HANA and ERPsim prior to event - no training is provided on the day of the event. An information and practice session will be offered one week prior to the event.

Each university may register one team of 3 to 5 students and one or two faculty coaches. The main coach is responsible to register the team. We will communicate with the team through the main coach. Diverse teams are encouraged.

Rules

  • Participation is FREE. Registration will close when team registrations are at capacity.
  • The event is offered in a virtual format.
  • The coach is the main point of contact for all event logistics and communications.
  • Students names and university email addresses are provided prior to the event.
  • The teams will play 4 rounds of the ERPsim Manufacturing Sustainability Game.
  • Each round will be of 20 virtual days of 1 minute each.
  • Teams will be ranked based on their company valuation.
  • No cheating is allowed! All teams must always behave ethically. The organizers reserve the rights to disqualify teams who are not playing fairly.

Prizes

  • Digital badges for the top 3 teams, the coaches, and the registered participants.
  • The top 3 teams qualify for the International ERPsim Final.

entry.title: Opentext SSL certificate deployment - onpremise
entry.content:
Deploying SSL certificate – Opentext On-premises.

Introduction:

In this blog you will learn about how to generate the keystore and with the help of keystore how to create CSR request then proceeding with importing the certificate response in the server. Here we are using the self signed SSL certificate signed by its own organisation with DNS entry in it.

SAP Extended Enterprise content Management 24.4 by Opentext for SAP S/4HANA is choosen by customer as their Document solution. 

Key consideration: Based on the requirement and compatibility we are using tomcat10.1 and JDK17

Terminology: OTDS - Opentext Directory Server

About Opentext:

OpenText in SAP is a document management and enterprise content management (ECM) solution used to store, manage, archive, and retrieve documents that are related to SAP business transactions.

It integrates with SAP systems to handle documents like invoices, purchase orders, contracts, HR files, and scanned documents in a secure and structured way.

Why is SSL certificate required?

To secure communication between the end user and the opentext server we do require the SSL certificate to be deployed in the server level.

Upon deploying the SSL certificate, we can make sure that

  1. Encryption will happen between user and server during data transfer
  2. User credentials and sensitive data will be protected.
  3. Corporate security standards will be maintained.
  4. No popups or warning signs will be visible in the browser session.

Procedure:

1. keystore generation:

Goto the path /usr/sap/<SID>/Java/jdk-17/bin and generate the keystore using the below command in the cmd prompt.

Enter the keystore password.

2. certificate request CSR generation.

Create the certificate request by the given command and make sure to provide the keystore password which was given above.

3. using any online decoder site you can check the generated CSR whether the SAN and other information’s are correct or not.

4. Make the necessary changes inside the server.xml file which is located inside the path E:/tomcat/conf/server.xml

5. Once you receive the certificate from your IT team start deploying the root certificate first and then the server certificate.

Follow the below command and make sure to enter the keystore password.

Type yes for trusting the certificate.

Now install the server certificate which is signed by IT team.

Now the certificate has been deployed in the server.

6. Restart the tomcat service.

7. Make sure the certificate is deployed and the url gets launch via https

8. Now try to launch the opentext OTDS admin url via https.

Note: Make sure the https port is allowed inside the firewall.

Issues faced: While importing the response certificate, it was not properly installed in the keystore. After regenerating the keystore and reimporting the certificate, it was installed successfully.

Conclusion:

Now, you are able to understand how you create the CSR using the keystore which has been generated and importing the response in the opentext server. This article has been prepared based on our project experience and the solution has been verified to be working. It is very important to check the compatibility version before deploying the certificate.

Thanks

Balaji

entry.title: Business Partner @ SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public & Private Edition - Solution Scope and Adoption Planning
entry.content:
Introduction

This blog is part of my small blog series Business Partner @ SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.
In this post, we’re moving into the Prepare phase of the SAP Activate methodology — the point where we define and sharpen the project scope.



In this step, the goal is to refine and align the solution scope based on the Digital Discovery Assessment (DDA) and previous pre‑sales conversations. Ultimately, we set a clear baseline: What will be delivered in the first waves, and which topics will be postponed for later adoption?
If you want more details on Scoping, you’ll find everything in SAP Activate via the Road Map Viewer (SAP for Me).
Navigation: Content → Prepare → Solution Scope and Adoption Planning

Scope Items

Here are the most relevant Scope Items:

  • Accounts Receivable (J59)
  • Accounts Payable (J60)
  • Mass Load and Mass Maintenance for Business Partners (7MI)

Depending on the project, you might require additional Scope Items. However, based on my experience, these three are the most relevant and commonly used.

SAP Signavio Process Navigator 

At this point, we switch from the SAP Activate methodology over to the process perspective in SAP Signavio Process Navigator  to take a deeper dive into the underlying processes.

One small drawback: there is currently no dedicated Scope Item for the generic Business Partner setup.

To access the latest process versions, simply use the search field:



In my example, I searched for J59. The results show the corresponding Solution Process across all available releases, including Solution Scenarios (Public and Private) and available countries.

Just select the entry that matches your system landscape and you can drill down into the details.

Accounts Receivable (J59)

When you open the Solution Process, the detailed view appears.



To identify the step that touches the Business Partner, simply click on the higher‑level process step highlighted in red.



Accounts Payable (J60)

Opening the Solution Process brings you directly to the detailed process view.



Here as well, the Business Partner–relevant step can be found by selecting the higher‑level step marked in red.



Mass Load and Mass Maintenance for Business Partners (7MI)

Clicking the Solution Process displays the full details.


Conclusion


With these steps, you can easily locate all Business‑Partner‑related content inside SAP Signavio Process Navigator, including:

  • Process Flow
  • Accelerators
  • Process Description

entry.title: Chinese fonts are not shown as expected
entry.content:
In a form the fonts Kozuka Gothic® Pr6N B are used and they do not show the chinese  characters as you expect them in the form.

 

Kozuka Gothic® Pr6N B is not a chinese font its a japanese font. Asian fonts are different depending on the country indeed.

The free to use fonts you find here:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_BUSINESS_BYDESIGN/2754875d2d2a403f95e58a41a9c7d6de/d0ccf70030584d24b5ca75ec264f2776.html

 

Ensure your font is matching with the country and language.

Chinese characters are different then Japanese once.

entry.title: Re: How to use BTP Destination with REST API in UI5 App (...
entry.content:
@TimurKalynovskyi 

When testing in BAS, UI5 framework uses the configuration in ui5.yaml for connecting to external services (not xs-app.json), and the path has to start with the same pattern as the path used for calling the service. 

Therefore, the following URL suits the current setting in the ui5.yaml. If the endpoint really starts with /testproject1, then you have to adjust the ui5.yaml accordingly.

url: "/backend/application/api/v1/documenthub/$metadata"

The internal server error (500) typically occurs when the credentials maintained in the destination are incorrect. Please note that the connection test from the destination only validates if the service is reachable, and it does not validate whether the authentication is successful.

For the Asset History Sheet S_ALR_87011990, the report picks up values based on the Sort Variant selected.
For example, if you are using Sort Variant 0001, you can review its configuration by following the steps below:

  1. Go to T‑code OAVI
  2. Double‑click on Sort Variant 0001
  3. The system will display the corresponding table and field assignments

Similarly, if you need to change the Asset History Sheet version, you can do so via:

  1. Go to T‑code OA79
  2. Add or modify the Asset History Sheet version as required

entry.title: Create, Delete and Updating of Packing Specification(PACKSPEC) through Upload Program Report
entry.content:
Introduction:

Maintaining of Packing Specification(Minimum Order Quantity and Target Quantity) for every Material-Plant combination in Service Parts Planning(SPP) system is a crucial step. Usually we do that through Standard T-Code /SCWM/IPU through upload file.

Instead to improve user experience and simplify the process of uploading, we created an Upload Report Program.

Body:

Development Completed on April 4 , 2025

Usually, creating, deleting or updating Packspec in T-Code /SCWM/PACKSPEC is a time-consuming process and requires lot of manual efforts to change every packspec directly in the standard screen.

This can also be done in T-Code /SCWM/IPU using upload template, but that is not user-friendly which requires us to download standard template of more than 100 columns and wants us to edit each and every column and upload. This is quite difficult.

It will also create a new packspec every time in the system, which will also increase the count of No. of Packspec in system, and which is also not consolidated with respect to Minimum Quantity-Target Quantity or plant-material combo. This makes us to go for custom upload program which is quite efficient in analyzing the Minimum Quantity-Target Quantity, Plant-material combination and decides to craete/update packspec based on user input.

Our Requirement is to reduce the manual intervention of changing the packspec directly in standard screen or reduce the complexity structure of upload file, instead we can have an upload program which does the Create, Update or Delete processes and provide results of the progress in SLG1 log and also as ALV report for end-users.

General Overview about Packing Specification:

One Packspec number consists of many condition records. Each Condition record represents one product and one location. 

Business users usually maintains set of material-plant combo under one specific packspec number with MOQ(Minimum Order Quantity)(Minimum Quantity for Supplier - SAP system identification name) and UOQ(Unit Order Quantity)( Target Quantity - SAP system identification name).

They will also be changing this from time-to-time as per business changes.

Detailed Overview about our Report Prorgam:

Requirement consists of 2 mode namely Manual Mode and Full Mode.

  1. Manual Mode is to change only the MOQ and UOQ of the packspec number, instead of keeping hands on the condition records. Suppose if business wants to change MOQ. UOQ from 2,3 to 6,2 respectively for all the same set of mat-loc combo present in the packspec number, they can use this manual mode to change only the MOQ and UOQ.
  2. Full Mode will consume upload file as input. Upload Template:
    MaterialPlantDEPL/DRPMOQUOQMode A/D
  3. From inputs from this file, it will check mode first as A/D.
  4. Based on that, it checks if the material-loc combo is present under any other packspec number, if yes, program will delete existing condition record and create a new one with given MOQ and UOQ.
  5. Above is example of one scenario, like this it should work for every possible scenario.
  6. Delete a packspec , if suppose the last condition record in packspec is requested to be deleted.
  7. Create a new packspec if a new set of MOQ-UOQ is requested n upload file.
  8. Add one more condition record, if the requested MOQ-UOQ is already present in System.
Arriving at Solution:
  1. We need to debug the standard T-code /SCWM/IPU to find all the standard FM's used to create, update and delete Packspec . FM's used to create, change, delete CONDITION RECORD is also required.
  2. Function Modules found:
    • ‘/SCWM/API_PACKSPEC_READ’ - To read the existing packspec details
    • '/SCWM/API_PACKSPEC_CREATE' - To create a new Packspec
    • '/SCWM/API_PACKSPEC_2ND_VERSION' - To create 2nd version of packspec (i.e) make it inactive, add required condition records.
    • '/SCWM/API_PACKSPEC_CHANGE' - To change the MOQ and UOQ of the packspec
    • '/SCWM/API_PACKSPEC_ACTIVATE' - To activate the inactive packspec.
    • '/SCWM/API_PACKSPEC_DELETE' - To Delete the whole packspec
    • '/SCWM/API_CONDITION_REPLICATE' - To create only one condition record within pakspec
    • '/SCWM/PS_DELETE_CONDTECH' - To delete only condition record, if last condition record, call packspec delete FM also.
    • '/SAPCND/MNT_SAVE_OW' - After deleting the condition record, call this FM to make the deletion process complete...as this is the process followed in standard process for successful deletion.
    • 'OWN_LOGICAL_SYSTEM_GET' - To get the logical system number.
  3. Tables used:
    • '/1CN/CPSCUS_SPP' - To get packspec details of Deployment Packspec.
    • '/1CN/CPSCUS_PRPL ' - To get packspec details of DRP Packspec.
    • '/SCWM/PNPAKH' - Pack Spec Header(ippe node)
    • 'PALTID' - iPPE Alternative: Time-Independent ID and Attributes
    • '/SCWM/PAPAK' - packaging specification level (ippe alternative)
  4. We created a report named "ZSPPR_PACKSPEC_UPLOAD" with selection screen to run the program and a backend global class named "ZCL_PS_MASTER" to support the required logics needed.
  5. ZSPPR_PACKSPEC_UPLOADZCL_PS_MASTER global class
  6. Report consists of Top declarations, Selection screen logics including download the template of upload file, Modification ID to differentiate between manual and full mode , Program flow with object for ZCL_PS_MASTER and flow logic and forms with different set of operations involved like change_moq_uoq, upload_data_from excel_to_itab, data_validation, refresh_depl_table, refresh_drp_table, add_mode, delete_mode, Write_log, calculate_pass_fail records for summary, display manual ALV and display Full ALV output as result.
  7. Importing data from Excel to ITAB can be done using Standard FM like CL_GUI_FRONTEND_SERVICES=>GUI_UPLOAD, but this method has errors of terminating the whole SAP GUI session while trying to close the opened Excel file by our program.
  8. To resolve this we came up with solution of importing set of custom classes ‘ZCL_EXCEL_READER_2007' into a separate package. This is available in abap2xlsx github repository. GitHub - abap2xlsx/abap2xlsx: Generate your professional Excel spreadsheet from ABAP · GitHub 
  9. With help of ABAPGIT Standalone program , we were able to add this set of classes into a new package.  Installation | abapGit Docs 
  10. Using method GET_CELL of class ‘ZCL_EXCEL_READER_2007' , we will get all the data in excel to ITAB, without popping up of Excel GUI explicitly, avoiding unwanted termination of SAP GUI.
  11. Once these classes are installed, we can make use multiple times in our future upload programs.
  12. ALV display is enhanced with colored outputs with green/red for users to identify the error records easily.
  13. The below logic is used to identify that given line of Packspec comes under which condition of Add/Delete Packspec.
    • [MODE - A]
    • IF  MOQ-UOQ from excel is present in system.
    •       IF MAT-LOC from excel is in the same MOQ-UOQ from EXCEL.
    •                   Throw ERROR"Same packspec combination already exists.".
    •       ELSEIF (Condition Record is present in system in another MOQ-UOQ).
    •                   Delete that Condition Record from existing packspec, create new condition record under given MOQ-UOQ.     
    •       ELSE.
    •                   Add the MAT-LOC to the given packspec.
    •       ENDIF.
    • ELSE.
    •       IF Condition Record is present in system.
    •                   Delete that Condition Record from existing packspec, create a new PACKSPEC.
    •       ELSE.
    •                   Create a new PACKSPEC.                                                                         
    • ENDIF.
    • [MODE - D].
    • IF MOQ-EOQ from excel is present in system.
    •       IF MAT-LOC from excel is in the same MOQ-EOQ from EXCEL.
    •                   IF No. of Condition Records = 1
    •                               Delete the PACKSPEC.
    •                   ELSE.
    •                               Delete the Condition Record from PACKSPEC.
    •                   ENDIF.
    •       ELSE.
    •                   Condition record is not under the given MOQ-UOQ.
    •       ENDIF.
    • ELSE.
    •       Packspec(MOQ-UOQ) does not exist in system-THROW error.
    • ENDIF.
  14. Technical Mindmap for the program flow:
  16.  Screenshots of Manual Mode Test run ALV, Real run ALV and SLG1 log output.
  17. Test Run ALV OutputReal Run ALV OutputReal Run SLG1 log Output

    Screenshots of Full mode Test run ALV, Real run ALV and SLG1 log output.

    Test Run ALV OutputReal Run ALV OutputReal Run SLG1 Log output

    Validation check results Screenshot:

  18. Difficult part is to find the relationship between the tables , where the condition number is getting stored.
  19. While debugging, the condition record actually gets saved after another FM call of MNT_SAVE_OW, but to find that exact FM took really more time.
  20. Faced lot of difficulties in DB storage malfunction due to continuous execution of FM's, so found solution with COMMIT WORK and Wait upto 2 seconds. This resolved the DB overflow issue.
  21. Learned how to stop debugger exactly at CALL FUNCTION points while executing a t-code. 
  22. You can create SQVI query like below for your convenience at the time of development for testing purpose and data-validation!
  23.  SQVI query for Deployment packspecSQVI query for DRP packspec

Conclusion:

We can create upload reports in this way consisting of Minimum and Target Quantity with Plant-Location Combination with mode A/D. We can modify this according to business use-cases and include /exclude columns in upload file. We've kept changing parameters in TVARVC table and hard-coded the constant parameters.

This was developed when I had 8-9 months of experience, excuse me for best coding practices. Attached complete code for your reference! Please feel free to post your comments and message me for any support regarding this! Hope this helps someone! 😊

entry.title: Five Global Procurement Narratives and Their Strategic Drivers
entry.content:
Procurement rarely attracts attention until something breaks. A missed delivery. A supplier failure. A sudden price shock. These moments expose how dependent organisations have become on invisible networks of suppliers, data, and relationships. They also reveal something deeper: procurement is no longer a back‑office function. It has become a strategic discipline shaped by global pressures, rising expectations, and rapid technological change. 

Today, procurement leaders face five narratives that influence nearly every decision they make. Each one represents a shift in how organisations create value and manage risk. Together, they show why procurement now sits at the heart of long‑term organisational resilience.  

Digital Procurement Transformation: Rewriting the Operating Model 

Digital transformation has become a defining narrative for procurement teams worldwide. In many organisations, the old model of emails, spreadsheets, and manual approvals still lingers—often held in place by habit rather than strategy. Yet the cost of staying analogue grows every year. Delays, errors, and limited visibility quickly accumulate. 

Digital procurement changes the rhythm of work. It replaces guesswork with insight and turns slow, linear processes into faster and more reliable flows. Automating repetitive tasks frees teams to focus on strategic decisions. Real‑time analytics reveal patterns that used to be buried in scattered files. And faster approvals reduce friction for the entire organisation. 

One procurement manager recently described the difference in simple terms: “Before, I spent my time chasing numbers. Now I spend it understanding them.” It is a small story, but it captures the larger shift—a move from activity to impact. 

The strategic driver behind this narrative is the need for speed, clarity, and alignment. Organisations that modernise their procurement foundations can act faster, negotiate better, and respond more effectively to uncertainty. Those that do not risk being outpaced by competitors who understand the value of data‑driven decision‑making.  

Sustainable and ESG‑Focused Sourcing: Moving from Statements to Standards 

A second narrative has risen sharply in recent years: sustainability and ESG‑focused sourcing. What was once a peripheral concern has become a defining expectation for customers, regulators, employees, and investors. 

This shift is not simply about compliance. It reflects a broader redefinition of value. Organisations are judged not only by the quality of their products but by the behaviour of the suppliers behind them. Environmental impact, labour practices, resource use, and ethical conduct all form part of the procurement decision. 

The strategic driver here is accountability. Organisations must show how their supply chains contribute to a more sustainable economy—not just in annual reports, but in measurable actions. That includes new supplier standards, better transparency across supply tiers, and meaningful engagement with suppliers on improvement plans. 

The work is not easy. Some suppliers lack the capability to meet new expectations. Others may resist change. But the organisations that succeed are those that treat ESG not as a reporting exercise but as an opportunity to strengthen resilience, innovation, and trust. 

A brief moment of doubt often appears here. Some leaders worry that sustainability will increase costs or slow delivery. Yet, in practice, many organisations discover the opposite: responsible suppliers are often more reliable, more efficient, and more aligned with long‑term strategy. 

Supply Chain Resilience and Risk Management: Preparing for a Permanent State of Disruption 

If the past decade has taught procurement anything, it is that disruption is no longer unusual—it is normal. Political instability, logistical bottlenecks, climate‑related events, and shifting market conditions continually test the limits of global supply networks. 

Resilience has therefore become a central narrative, driven by the need to anticipate and absorb shocks. It demands visibility beyond first‑tier suppliers. It requires diversified sources, scenario planning, and early‑warning indicators. And it relies on honest collaboration with suppliers who understand that resilience is a shared responsibility. 

A small example illustrates this well. During a recent regional disruption, one supplier notified its customer of a potential delay nearly two weeks before it became public. That early signal allowed the buying organisation to activate alternative supply routes and avoid costly downtime. It was not technology alone that made this possible—it was the relationship behind it. 

The strategic driver of this narrative is continuity. Organisations need to operate with confidence even when conditions change. Resilience is no longer optional; it is a foundation for strategic stability. 

Strategic Supplier Collaboration and Innovation: Building Value Beyond the Contract 

The fourth narrative centres on the growing recognition that suppliers are not just vendors—they are partners in capability, innovation, and shared growth. As supply chains become more complex, no organisation can solve its challenges alone. The expertise, creativity, and capacity for innovation often sit outside the organisation, within supplier networks. 

This narrative emphasises collaboration over control. It involves sharing information, co‑developing solutions, and building long‑term relationships. When done well, supplier collaboration becomes a multiplier for innovation. It shortens development cycles, improves quality, and helps organisations solve problems before they escalate. 

The strategic driver here is mutual value. Organisations that treat suppliers as partners rather than cost centres see gains in agility, performance, and competitive differentiation. They create an ecosystem rather than a transactional marketplace. 

 Cost Optimisation and Value Maximisation: Evolving Beyond Traditional Savings 

Cost will always matter. But the definition of cost—what it includes, what it measures, and what it influences—has expanded. Procurement teams are increasingly expected to look beyond price and consider total value: quality, reliability, risk, sustainability, and long‑term impact. 

Cost optimisation is now about smarter decisions, not simply lower numbers. It involves using analytics to identify patterns of waste, consolidating categories where appropriate, and designing contracts that balance flexibility with performance. 

The strategic driver behind this narrative is the need for sustainable value creation. Organisations cannot cut their way to long‑term growth. They must balance immediate savings with resilience, innovation, and strategic alignment. 

Where Technology Creates Practical Bridges: The Subtle Role of SAP and SAP Ariba 

Across all five narratives, technology plays an enabling role—but not the only one. Modern procurement platforms such as SAP S/4HANA and/or SAP Ariba ecosystems offer practical support where organisations need it most: visibility, consistency, and collaboratio 

These tools help teams manage supplier data more effectively, streamline sourcing, and monitor risks in real time. They also provide a structured environment for managing contracts, analysing spend, and integrating ESG considerations into everyday decisions. 

What matters is not the technology itself but what organisations do with it. The tools simply make it easier to embed good practices into daily work. When used well, they help procurement teams act with clarity and confidence—especially when dealing with complexity, uncertainty, and global scale. 

 A Synthesis: Five Narratives, One Direction 

Although each narrative is distinct, they converge around a common theme: procurement has become a strategic driver of organisational resilience. 

  • Digital capabilities enable speed and insight. 
  • Sustainability strengthens reputation and long‑term trust. 
  • Resilience reduces vulnerability. 
  • Supplier collaboration nurtures innovation. 
  • Value‑based cost management ensures financial discipline without sacrificing quality or purpose. 

What ties them together is a shift from reactive procurement to strategic procurement. A shift from transactions to relationships. From information scarcity to insight. From cost‑cutting to long‑term value creation. 

The five narratives are not trends to observe. They are forces to respond to—often simultaneously. 

 Ending: Returning to the Opening Tension 

In the beginning, we considered how procurement only becomes visible when something breaks. But understanding these five narratives changes that picture. Procurement becomes visible not because of failure, but because of its strategic importance. 

The tension remains—but it shifts. Instead of worrying about the next disruption, organisations begin to ask a different question: How can procurement shape the future rather than simply react to it? 

The answer lies in embracing these narratives with clarity, commitment, and curiosity. When organisations do that, procurement stops being the invisible backbone and becomes a deliberate force for resilience, responsibility, and long‑term value. 

Kind regards

Lars Elmquist

entry.title: SAP Digital Manufacturing: Creating Your First SAP DM PPD
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

At the beginning of my journey with SAP Digital Manufacturing through SAP Learning Hub, I encountered several challenges, particularly because many courses required prior knowledge or setup of OPC UA connections or the use of REST APIs.

To help others avoid facing similar difficulties, I have written this blog to provide a simplified starting point. In this post, I demonstrate how to get started with Production Process Designer (PPD) using a basic use case, where the only prerequisite is that a production order has already been created.


In this blog I will try to build a simple use case where we will try to release the order automatically once an order is created.

In this blog, we will develop a foundational understanding of key concepts within SAP Digital Manufacturing’s Production Process Designer (PPD), including:

  • Local Variables
  • Loops
  • Script Tasks
  • Conditions
  • Triggers

    These topics will be explored to provide a structured and practical learning experience for beginners.

Step-by-Step Implementation in Production Process Designer (PPD)

Create a New Production Process

  • Open the Design Production Process application.
  • Click on Create.
  • Select the Create Process icon to define a new production process.

 

Add Order Retrieval Service
  • Add the OrderList V2 service.
    • This service retrieves all orders that are yet to be released.
  • Maintain the following input parameters:
    • releaseStatuses (StringArray): Enter "RELEASABLE" to filter only releasable orders.
    • plant: Enter the plant ID (e.g., 1134).

 

 

Configure Local Variables
  • Navigate to Local Variables and create:
    • Type: Structured Array
    • Schema: GetOrderListV2Response
    • Order_List
  • Purpose:
    • This variable stores the service output containing all unreleased orders.
  • Map the service output to Order_List.
  • Connect the service key with the end icon.

 

  • Save, deploy, and execute the production process.
  • Use Monitor Production Processes to verify the output.

 

 

 
Process Retrieved Orders Using Script Task
  • Add a Script Task to transform the structured array and give us a single order output.

Input Parameter:

  • Input_Orders
    • Type: Structured Array
    • Schema: GetOrderListV2Response

Output Parameters:

  • Releaseable_Orders (StringArray)
  • Total_Orders (Integer)

Local Variables:

  • Order (String)
  • O_Iteration_Len (Integer)

 

 

 

Script Logic:

 

var orders = $input.Input_Orders;

var releaseableOrders = [];



for (var i = 0; i < orders.length; i++) {

    releaseableOrders.push(orders[i].order);

}



$output.Releaseable_Orders = releaseableOrders;

$output.Total_Orders = orders.length;

Test run

Implement Loop for Order Processing

  • Create a local variable:
    • O_Index (Integer) with default value 0.
  • Add a Condition Control with the expression: O_Index < O_Iteration_Len

 

Extract Individual Orders

  • Add a Script Task to iterate through orders.

Input Parameters:

  • I (Integer)
  • Order_List (StringArray)

Output Parameter:

  • Order (String)

Local Variable:

  • Order_Iteration (String)

 

 

 

Script:

$output.Order_Iteration = $input.Order_List[$input.I];
  • Test using sample inputs:
    • Order List: ["400000000182","400000000183","400000000184","400000000185"]
    • Index: 2

Release Orders

  • Connect the script task to Release_Order_In_Background service.

Inputs:

  • Order Key: Order_Iteration
  • Plant: 1134

 

Increment Loop Counter

  • Add another Script Task to increment the index.

Input:

  • Input_Index (Integer)

Output:

  • Output_Index (Integer)

 

 

Script: 

$output.Output_Index = $input.Input_Index + 1;

Connect this back to the Condition Control to complete the loop.

Execute and Validate

  • Run the production process.
  • Use debugging tools if errors occur.
  • Verify that orders are successfully released.

Deploy and Automate

  • Click Edit Header.
  • Enable Publish to Service Registry.
  • Save and deploy.

 

Configure Automatic Trigger

  • Navigate to Manage Automatic Triggers.
  • Select Business Rules.
  • Create the rule on the event Order Created and assign the created process in action items.
  • Perform Quick Deploy.

 

 

Final Outcome

Once configured, this automation ensures that:

  • Any order transferred from SAP S/4HANA to Digital Manufacturing will be automatically released without manual intervention
