entry.title: Completed order lignes without expected quantity and without a rejection reason
entry.content:
Hello SAP experts,

I am facing an issue in SAP and would appreciate your guidance.

In a sales order, a line item is automatically set to status “Completed”, even though:

  • the delivered quantity is lower than the ordered quantity, and
  • no rejection reason is applied on the item.

I am trying to understand the root cause behind this system behavior.

My question is:
How can I identify WHY SAP decided to close the line?

Any guidance on how to analyze this step‑by‑step (or which transactions / tables / logs to check) would be very helpful.

Thank you in advance for your support!

entry.title: Re: Implementing Custom Logic to Include Project Informat...
entry.content:
Good day @Rohit1105 

Thank you for your question.

The guide you referenced addresses the Public Cloud extensibility example that allows you to add project information to the billing email template header. However, SAP uses Sales: Sales Document with Sales Header modification for the logic that fills the sales document header custom field before it is forwarded to billing.

Consult these references for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_EXTENSIBILITY_EXPLORER/397bf26514884cc8a5ece65970e8e3e6/42bddfd5840c4e0187abaaef630e64bb.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a376cd9ea00d476b96f18dea1247e6a5/66a2f4107e754be68a458408eb332d91.html?locale=en-US

So, for your exact use case, you need to create the custom logic in Sales and the extension point you should select is:

  • For Business Context: Sales: Sales Document
  • For Business Object Node: Sales Order Header
  • For Extension Point/BAdI: Modification of Header Custom Fields in Sales Documents (SD_SLS_MODIFY_HEAD)

For your scenario, you must implement the logic on sales document header, not on an item node and not on the billing document output BAdI for your email scenario. It is important to note that Sales: Billing Document output form BAdI is for enhancing the PDF output, whereas your email header scenario is handled through Sales: Sales Document/Sales Header Modification.

See the following references for more details.

Add the Project ID to the Email Template Header

Extensibility for Output Forms and Email Templates of Billing Documents

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Dump When Checking Faulty RAP Object Based On Union CDS-View (OnPremise 2023 SP01)
entry.content:
Dear Community,

Recently I found a bug when checking/activating a RAP object which is based on a union CDS-View and has an error across multiple artifacts. I would like to describe and summarize this for reporting and to save other people’s time during analysis.

Initial Situation

Let's say I want to consolidate different findings from our beloved sflight. For this purpose, I created the following union view:

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [ #NONE ]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Unifies different validation findings'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true

define root view entity ZI_FLIGHT_FINDINGS_UNION
  as select from ZI_DELAYED_FLIGHTS

{
  key Carrid,
  key Connid,
  key Fldate,

      ValidationResult,
      ValidationCategory,
      DelayInMinutes,
      -1                  as MaxPassengers,
      -1                  as BookedPassengers
}

union
  select from ZI_OVERBOOKED_FLIGHTS

{
  key Carrid,
  key Connid,
  key Fldate,

      ValidationResult,
      ValidationCategory,
      -1                  as DelayInMinutes,
      MaxPassengers,
      BookedPassengers
}

Based on that union, I want to create a simple Fiori Elements app in which the user can see all the different findings and is able to create comments via an action. For the action we create the following Behavior Definition:

unmanaged implementation in class zbp_i_flight_findings_union unique;
strict ( 2 );

define behavior for ZI_FLIGHT_FINDINGS_UNION

lock master
authorization master ( instance )

{
  action addComment parameter char200;
}

 I decided to go for an unmanaged implementation, because unions do not easily support CRUD operations (and they are not needed for the business case yet). The handler class in the class pool looks like this:

CLASS lhc_ZI_FLIGHT_FINDINGS_UNION DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_handler.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    METHODS get_instance_authorizations FOR INSTANCE AUTHORIZATION
      IMPORTING keys REQUEST requested_authorizations FOR zi_flight_findings_union RESULT result.

    METHODS addComment FOR MODIFY
IMPORTING keys FOR ACTION zi_flight_findings_union~addComment.

    METHODS read FOR READ
      IMPORTING keys FOR READ zi_flight_findings_union RESULT result.
ENDCLASS.


CLASS lhc_ZI_FLIGHT_FINDINGS_UNION IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD get_instance_authorizations.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD addComment.
    "Create comment by EML ....
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD read.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

Everything works, everything can be activated. But now silly me wants to save additional data from our ZI_FLIGHT_FINDINGS_UNION object. Reading with EML isn't possible yet, because read isn't implemented. To avoid implementing the read method myself, I got the idea to change the Behavior Definition to managed with unmanaged save (would this even work?):

managed with unmanaged save implementation in class zbp_i_flight_findings_union unique;
strict ( 2 );

define behavior for ZI_FLIGHT_FINDINGS_UNION
lock master
authorization master ( instance )
{
  action addComment parameter char200;
}

Now I introduced some inconsistencies, because the handler still contains the read method.
But these errors are hidden now.

There is no warning inside the behavior definition, but activating leads to the following error:

 

By checking ST22 we can see that we get for every syntax check and every activation the following dump:

 

If we don't know the mistake we made inside the behavior definition and its implementation we have no indication of what went wrong.

How this dump happens

The dump happens inside the Class CL_CSP_MD_ANNOTATION_READER in method create_by_metadata:

    lo_instance->mt_element_annotation = CORRESPONDING #( is_cds_annotations_md-elements MAPPING element_name     = elementname
                                                                                                 annotation_name  = annoname
                                                                                                 annotation_value = value ).

This statement tries to write values from is_cds_annotations_md-elements to lo_instance->mt_element_annotation.  That table expects a unique key with annotation_name and element_name.

 

But this uniqueness is not given:

 

The reason is in table DDFIELDANNO in which the assigned annotations are stored. The annotation ABAPCATALOG.INTERNAL.ISMANDT is stored twice, because of our union: 

Workaround

Caution: Be careful when working in this area, especially when debugging or activating the object. I cannot guarantee that it won’t lead to inconsistencies or other issues in your system. You do everything at your own risk.

At the end of method IF_CSP_WRP_DD_ANNOTATION~GET_ANNOS_MASS in class CL_CSP_WRP_DD_ANNOTATION I placed a Breakpoint, triggered the check (not the activation!) and was able to remove the duplicate line. After that, I was able to get the real finding:

"Class can't be analyzed during BDEF implementation check. Inconsistencies or errors exist: "ZI_FLIGHT_FINDINGS_UNION" has the implementation type "managed", hence the implementation type "READ" is superfluous."

Conclusion

With this workaround it's possible to avoid the dump and retrieve the actual finding.

To fix the dump, the duplicate entry must be prevented. I assume that either the selection (method _GET_DRCT_ANNS_4_ENTT_ELMNTS_M in class CL_DD_DDL_ANNOTATION_SERVICE) or the merging of the annotations (method MERGE_ENTITIES_ELMNTS_ANNOS in class CL_DD_DDL_ANNOTATION_MERGER) might be the relevant place to fix it.

I'm also creating an incident and keep you updated. If it's already fixed after 2023 SP01, please let me know.

Thank you for reading and best regards
Marc

entry.title: Re: Learning Hub Subscription Purchasing Error (VAL_0101)
entry.content:
Hi @CezaryG 

Has your issue been resolved?
Kind regards
Margit

entry.title: Re: IoT: Air Quality Dashboard with SAP RAP and Mobile App
entry.content:
Hello @vsubbakrishna ,

Thanks a lot for your feedback, really appreciate it! 

Since this is a small project, a lot of things were intentionally simplified or left out.

A more robust and scalable architecture would look like this:

EDGE Device (e.g. Raspberry Pi + sensor)

1) IoT / Integration Layer

  • SAP BTP (e.g. Integration Suite, Event Mesh)
  • or external broker (MQTT, REST API via Node.js / Python)

2) CAP / Node.js service (or Python microservice)

  • handles ingestion
  • validates & normalizes data

3) ABAP Cloud (RAP / OData V4)

  • stores business data
  • exposes it via OData

4) Consumption layer

  • SAP Fiori / SAPUI5 app
  • SAP MDK mobile app

entry.title: NPS Wage Type (/3FS) Not Posting to Both Debit and Credit in FI Integration
entry.content:
Hello All, 

We have a requirement wherein the NPS contribution wage type /3FS should be posted to both Debit (Expense GL) and Credit (Liability GL) during FI posting.

However, currently the system is posting the amount only to the Credit side (₹21,000) under the liability account. No corresponding Debit (expense) entry is being generated.

Observations:

  • The FI document is balanced (total debit = total credit).
  • No errors are encountered during posting (via PC00_M99_CIPE).
  • The posting run is completed successfully.
  • Wage type /3FS is processed in payroll but only reflected on the credit side in FI.

Expected Result:

  • Wage type /3FS should generate:
    • Debit Entry → Expense GL (Employer Contribution / NPS Expense)
    • Credit Entry → Liability GL (NPS Payable)

Request:
Kindly guide on the required configuration changes to achieve the above requirement.
Best Regards
Nitin

entry.title: SAP FI Deep Dive: Understanding BP–Vendor Synchronization in S/4HANA
entry.content:
When organizations move from ECC to S/4HANA, one of the most crucial shifts in accounting is the business partner (BP) approach. Unlike ECC, where Vendor (LFA1) and Customer (KNA1) masters were created separately, S/4HANA introduces a unified master data model, and with that comes the need to properly configure and synchronize BP → Vendor.


As an SAP FI consultant, mastering this synchronization is essential, especially if you support AP, AR, P2P, O2C, vendor onboarding workflows, or integrations.

  • Why BP-Vendor synchronization exists
  • End-to-end technical architecture
  • Configuration sequence (with screenshot markers)
  • Table-level understanding
  • Real-world issues & how to fix them

Best practices for enterprise rollout
1. Why Business Partner Became Mandatory in S/4HANA

The following factors drive the shift from separate vendor/customer masters to business partners:

  • A single source of truth for legal entity data
  • Consistent governance across FI, MM, SD, and external integrations
  • Partner roles making it easier to manage multi-role entities (e.g., a company that is both a vendor and a customer)
  • Better support for MDG, AIF, and workflow-based onboarding

Vendor (FI-MM) and Customer (FI-SD) are now simply BP roles.

2. BP Vendor Architecture Overview

  • BP (BUT000)
  • BP Role: FLVN00 – FI Vendor (Accounting)
  • BP Role: FLVN01 – Supplier (Purchasing)
  • Vendor Master (LFA1, LFB1, LFM1)

S/4HANA Logic 

  • BP General Data → BUT000
  • BP FI Vendor Role → LFA1 (General)
  • BP Company Code Accounting → LFB1
  • BP Purchasing → LFM1

3. Configuration Sequence (With screenshot placeholders)

Here is the exact sequence you should follow to ensure BP & Vendor sync without errors.

Step 1: Define BP Number Ranges

Path: SPRO → Cross-Application Components → SAP Business Partner → Business Partner → Basic Settings → Number Ranges

 
 

Step 2:  Define BP Grouping

The BP Grouping configuration displays the grouping key, description, and control of internal and external number assignment.

Step 3:  Assign Number Range to Grouping

The number range CK is assigned to the grouping ZCK, ensuring alignment between BP and vendor numbering.

Step 4: Define Vendor Account Groups

SPRO → Financial Accounting → Accounts Payable → Vendor Accounts → Master Data → Define Account Groups

Vendor Account Group Configuration: showing different account groups used to control vendor master data, field status, and number range assignment.

Step 5:  Assign Vendor Number Ranges to Account Groups (OBAS/XKN1)

Step 6:  Activate BP-Vendor Synchronization

SPRO → Cross-Application Components → Master Data Synchronization → Customer/Vendor Integration → Synchronization Control

4. How synchronization works internally (Table level)

  • BUT000 – BP Header
  • BUT020 – Address
  • BUT000-BKVEN → Vendor flag
  • LFA1 – Vendor General Data
  • LFB1 – Company Code Data
  • LFM1 – Purchasing Data

Key System Objects

  • CVI Link Table → CVI_CUST_LINK / CVI_VEND_LINK
  • Mapping Tables → CVI_FS_CHECKS, CVI_MAPPING

5. Common real-world issues and how to resolve them.

  • Error: “Vendor not created:  No BP role FLVN00/FLVN01

    Ensure BP Grouping ↔ Vendor Account Group mapping is correct



  • Error: “Number range mismatch (BP vs Vendor)”

    Ensure BP Grouping ↔ Vendor Account Group mapping is correct

    OBAS, BUBD, BUCG must align

  • Error in BP Sync: “CVI Mapping Incomplete”

    Run:   MDS_PPO2 – Error logs/MDS_LOAD_COCKPIT – Synchronization/CVICHECK – Consistency checks

6. Best Practices from real project experience 

  • Always maintain same internal number ranges for BP & Vendor to avoid sync failures
  • Synchronize legacy ECC vendors using MDS_LOAD_COCKPIT
  • Perform CVI pre-checks before conversion
  • Align field statuses across BP, Vendor & MDG
  • Use AIF if you have interfaces (Ariba, Coupa, xSuite)
  • Build Fiori approval workflows for Vendor onboarding

7. Final Thoughts

BP–Vendor synchronization is more than configuration—it is the foundation of all FI-MM processes in S/4HANA:

  • P2P (PR → PO → GR → IR)
  • Vendor Payments (F110, DMEEX, BCM)
  • Vendor Onboarding Workflows
  • 3rd-party integrations (Serengeti, Tangoe, Concur, xSuite)

8. Conclusion

The business partner approach in S/4HANA represents a fundamental shift in how master data is structured and managed compared to ECC. Proper BP–vendor synchronization is critical because it directly impacts vendor creation, invoice processing, payments, procurement, and system integrations. Understanding the configuration flow, number range alignment, CVI mapping, and synchronization tools is essential for every SAP FI consultant working in S/4HANA projects or support environments. When done right with good management, field mapping, and synchronization controls, the BP framework offers a central, flexible, and easy-to-integrate master data model that helps modern business processes and digital transformation efforts.

entry.title: Re: SAP Build Apps Deprecation and The Path Forward
SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting and Eightfold Integration
entry.content:
SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting and Eightfold Integration

If you’ve ever tried juggling multiple recruiting tools at once, you’ll know how messy things can get. SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting helps organize the hiring process, but it can feel a bit manual when it comes to finding the right candidates quickly. That’s where Eightfold comes in. It uses AI to really understand candidate skills and career paths.

When you connect SuccessFactors Recruiting with Eightfold, you essentially get structure and intelligence working together. SuccessFactors keeps everything clean and compliant, while Eightfold helps you spot the right talent much faster — and honestly, the combination removes a lot of the back‑and‑forth work recruiters get stuck with.

What is SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting?

Think of SuccessFactors Recruiting as the “home base” for all your hiring data. Job requisitions, budgets, org details, compliance, approvals — it all starts here.

Hiring managers create jobs in SuccessFactors, and recruiters move candidates through the typical stages like screening, interviews, feedback and selection.

The system is great for keeping everything consistent and accurate. The only catch? On its own, it still depends heavily on manual candidate matching. So while the process stays clean, it’s not always fast or intelligent when it comes to finding the right people.

What is Eightfold?

Eightfold works differently. Instead of focusing on job titles or keywords, it looks at skills, patterns in someone’s career, and a wide range of publicly available information. It builds a pretty rich picture of who someone is professionally — not just what’s written on their resume.

What’s helpful is that it can highlight people recruiters might have overlooked and reduce bias by focusing on capabilities instead of labels. It acts as a “system of intelligence” that sits on top of SuccessFactors and elevates the decision-making.

How the Integration Works

Once SuccessFactors Recruiting and Eightfold are connected, information flows back and forth automatically. The connector relies on SAP OData APIs and OAuth, so everything stays secure and synchronized.

Here's what the workflow looks like in plain English:

  1. Someone creates and approves a job requisition in SuccessFactors.
  2. That job shows up in Eightfold without anyone having to push a button.
  3. Recruiters do their deeper candidate discovery, matching, and engagement inside Eightfold.
  4. Any changes — like applicant status, submissions, or selections — automatically sync back into SuccessFactors.

No copying, no exporting, no double work. Both systems stay up to date on their own.

Benefits for Everyday Users

  • Less manual work: Automated screening can reduce time spent by up to 90%.
  • Better quality hires: AI-based skill matching improves the relevance of candidates.
  • Faster hiring: Companies often fill roles up to 35% faster.
  • More inclusive hiring: Skill‑based matching and unbiased recommendations help improve diversity.
  • Better candidate experience: Candidates receive personalized job recommendations.

Prerequisites

Before you start integrating, you’ll want to double‑check a few things:

  • SuccessFactors Recruiting needs to be active, and OData APIs must be enabled.
  • You’ll need admin access in both SuccessFactors Provisioning and Eightfold’s admin console.
  • Keep your SuccessFactors Company ID handy — you’ll need it while setting up OAuth credentials.

Step 1:

How to Enable OData APIs in SAP SuccessFactors

To enable OData APIs for integrating SuccessFactors with Eightfold, follow these steps:

  1. Log in to the SAP SuccessFactors Admin Center.
    Navigate to Admin Center → Manage OAuth2 Client Applications.
  2. Create a new OAuth2 client application for Eightfold.
    • Click Add New Application.
    • Enter a meaningful application name (e.g., Eightfold Integration).
    • Select the required Recruiting OData APIs, such as:
      • Job Requisitions
      • Job Applications
      • Candidates
      • Interview-related entities
  3. Generate the OAuth credentials.
    After saving, the system will provide a Client ID and Client Secret.
    Keep these secure—they will be needed in Eightfold for authentication.
  4. Confirm your OData endpoint.
    The typical endpoint format is:
  5. https://<companyId>.successfactors.com/odata/v2

Replace <companyId> with your organization’s actual SuccessFactors Company ID.

Step 2:

Configuring Eightfold to Connect with SAP SuccessFactors

  1. Go to the Integrations section in Eightfold.
    Navigate to Integrations → SAP SuccessFactors.
  2. Enter your SuccessFactors connection details.
    Provide the following information:
    • SuccessFactors Company ID
    • OAuth Client ID
    • OAuth Client Secret
  3. Select the data entities you want to sync.
    Typical entities include:
    • Job Requisitions
    • Positions
    • Applications
    • Candidates
    • Statuses (application status, disposition, etc.)
  4. Enable sync settings.
    • Turn on routine (scheduled) sync, such as every 30 minutes.
    • Enable real‑time webhooks for immediate updates when a job or candidate changes.

This setup ensures that SuccessFactors remains the source of truth, while Eightfold continuously receives fresh data for AI‑driven analysis and matching.

Step 3:

Before enabling the full sync between SuccessFactors and Eightfold, it's important to run a dry‑run test. This helps confirm that your credentials, permissions, and data mappings are set up correctly—without pushing any real changes into your production system.

Here’s how to perform the validation:

  1. Run a dry‑run sync in Eightfold.
    This test checks whether Eightfold is able to connect to SuccessFactors using the OAuth credentials you provided, but it does not import or modify any data.
  2. Monitor for common errors.
    During the dry‑run, look out for:
    • Missing or incorrect API permissions
    • Incorrect or outdated OAuth Client ID/Secret
    • Issues with field mappings, such as unexpected or unmapped SuccessFactors fields
  3. Validate sample data flow.
    After the dry‑run completes, confirm that:
    • A sample job requisition from SuccessFactors is successfully read by Eightfold
    • Entities like positions, candidates, and application statuses can be accessed without errors
  4. Address any failures before enabling the live sync.
    Fixing mapping or permission issues now prevents downstream sync failures once the system goes live.

 

Core Reasons for Integrating SuccessFactors with Eightfold

  • AI‑Driven Candidate Matching

Eightfold takes thousands of skills and career signals into account. It isn’t guessing — it’s comparing patterns. It shortlists strong candidates fast, including people who might not show up in a standard database search.

  • Real Automation

Instead of recruiters doing repetitive tasks all day, the integration automates résumé screening, candidate ranking, and data syncing. Many teams reclaim a huge amount of time — sometimes up to 90%.

  • Shorter Hiring Cycles

With better leads and less manual work, the entire cycle gets faster. Many organizations see a 30–35% improvement in time‑to‑hire.

  • Fairer & More Inclusive Hiring

Because Eightfold focuses on skills instead of keywords or titles, it often uncovers candidates who would otherwise be overlooked. It naturally encourages more diverse and equitable hiring.

entry.title: Why strategic leadership in product development will make or break your future
entry.content:
Imagine your company three years from now. New markets have opened, customer expectations have shifted overnight, and your competitors seem to release new products faster than you can schedule a cross‑department meeting. The pressure is rising, margins are shrinking, and decisions that once felt routine suddenly carry real weight.
Now imagine something else:
Every delay, every unclear requirement, every missing piece of information quietly expands into weeks of rework, growing costs, and lost opportunities. Not because your teams lack talent or commitment — but because the heartbeat of your business, product development, is running on outdated rhythms. This is the silent risk many organizations underestimate.

Product development is often seen as a technical domain — a place for engineers, specifications, and change requests. But in reality, it is where your future competitiveness is decided. The speed at which you can innovate, the consistency with which you execute, and the confidence with which you make decisions all begin here. When the product development engine falters, the entire business slows down. When it runs strategically, it accelerates everything around it.

Complexity persists, experience leaves

Generational shift hits digital reality
As experienced employees retire or leave the company, many organizations lose valuable product knowledge – or the knowledge remains fragmented, poorly documented, and therefore hardly usable. At the same time, the next generation expects modern work methods, transparency, and easy collaboration, also across departmental boundaries. Where processes and tools fail to meet these expectations, it’s not only efficiency that declines, but also the organization’s attractiveness as an employer.

Historically grown portfolios, lack of transparency
Product landscapes that have evolved over the years are often complex, highly variable, and difficult to control. Inconsistent data structures, complex change processes, and a lack of standards slow down innovation. Decision-making is based on assumptions rather than on reliable data. It is becoming increasingly difficult for the top management to reliably evaluate the risks, costs, and implications of product decisions.

Cost pressure, skilled labor shortage, overburdened IT
Product development is in the middle of a stress test. Cost pressure, skilled labor shortage, and overburdened IT departments are more and more defining the framework for product development. Rising development and regulatory costs meet limited resources: Qualified engineers and IT professionals are difficult to recruit, while IT departments are already fully occupied with operations, maintenance, and troubleshooting. At the same time, profit is diminishing due to increasing complexity, highly variable products, and inefficient processes.
This situation makes very clear: Product development can no longer be treated as merely an operational cost center.
Strategic steering is necessary instead – with clear goals, transparent governance, and measurable contributions to the company's success.

Focus on the big picture rather than isolated optimizations

Products, processes, and people are inseparably connected. Selective optimizations or isolated solutions don’t go far enough.

An integrative approach connects product development, business areas, and external partners, combining product data with commercial and operational information.
Only on this basis, systematic monitoring with clearly defined indicators becomes possible, thereby creating the foundation for well-informed, transparent decisions.

Measurable benefits

 

Strategic steering of product development: a management responsibility

Product development should not be seen as a cost center, but as a management task with strategic impact.
When companies rethink and purposefully manage their product development, they propel their entire business. This is reflected in the following aspects:

  • Better-informed decisions at board level through transparent, consistent, and comparable product data
  • Shorter time to market thanks to manageable changes that do not block processes
  • Higher profitability, as variety, costs, and risks become visible early on

This way, product development becomes a business driver – not a limiting factor.

How future-proof is your product development today – and tomorrow?
A self-assessment can provide valuable insights.

Management check: Where does your product development really stand? 

 

Utilizing complexity to promote innovation and growth

Complexity is not a product of chance – it is the logical result of market orientation. Products that are highly tailored to customer requirements automatically lead to a higher number of variants, dependencies, and changes.
At the same time, the complexity of the environment is growing due to distributed teams, global networks, external partners, and increasing regulatory requirements.
Inefficient processes, isolated systems, and unclear responsibilities additionally create “home-grown" complexity, which causes friction, delays, and a lack of transparency.

Today, it's not just about reducing complexity – mastering it systematically makes all the difference.

Organizations that create transparency about product structures, variants, dependencies, and changes maintain firm control even in complex environments.

This is how complexity becomes a competitive edge:

  • Variants are evaluated and controlled from an economic perspective at an early stage
  • Changes remain controllable, traceable, and scalable
  • Regulatory requirements are an integral part of product development, not a downstream obstacle
  • Home-grown complexity is systematically eliminated rather than managed through work-arounds

This turns product development into a powerful lever for speed, control, and sustainable success.

From vision to implementation: pragmatic and effective

How can good intentions be turned into real momentum in product development?
A clear starting point helps – and the following steps are particularly effective:

  • Align your product development consistently with the business goals.
  • Choose future-oriented, scalable software solutions tailored to your size, technological maturity, and available resources.
  • Focus on the core functions first – and gradually expand to the areas with the highest potential.
  • Work with experienced partners who combine industry expertise with a holistic end-to-end view.


Why SAP Is the right choice for strategic product development

SAP provides the foundation for a true end‑to‑end digital product lifecycle.
Organizations benefit from:

  • A unified platform for all engineering and product data
  • Standardized, transparent, and scalable lifecycle processes
  • Integration with ERP, supply chain, and manufacturing
  • Early supplier involvement and cross-company collaboration
  • Eliminated data silos through a continuous digital thread

This makes SAP software not just a system for managing data, but a strategic enabler for resilience, profitability, and long-term competitiveness.

Act today: Efficient product development secures your future

In times of profound change, product development becomes the central steering instrument. Only organizations that create transparency, master complexity, and intentionally shape the interaction between people, processes, and data can secure sustainable competitive advantages.

DSC Software AG supports companies on this journey – with a holistic, integrative approach to product lifecycle management based on SAP software. This approach regards product development as a business enabler and as a strategic investment in your company’s long-term viability.

Additional information on this topic and our contact details can be found here. 

@sean_laughlin @SarahMei @agrawal 

SAP Integrated Product Development SAP Engineering Control Center

entry.title: 🇩🇪 Build code-based AI Agents on SAP Business Technology Platform (Stuttgart, Germany)

In this CodeJam you can choose to build your agents with Python, CrewAI and LiteLLM or with JavaScript, LangGraph and LiteLLM.

The CodeJam will include the following SAP technologies:

- SAP RPT-1
- Grounding Service

entry.title: Re: Incomplete date handling - are incomplete dates conve...
entry.content:
This would be the default and expected, CR can't assume the format is valid.

You could try enabling CRLogger, CR's database logging component, to see what is being returned to verify ODBC is converting it to null:

To use
crlogger to trace Crystal Reports (per SAP Note 1603398), set Windows environment variables to enable logs and specify a directory. Create a C:\logging folder, add LOGGING_DIR=C:\logging, LOGGING_ENABLED_ASSERT=1, and LOGGING_ENABLED_RUNTIME=100 in System Properties, then restart Crystal Reports to generate crw32_*.log files. 
Steps to Enable crlogger Tracing:
  1. Create Log Directory: Create a folder named logging on your C: drive (C:\logging).
  2. Set Environment Variables:
    • Open Windows search, type "environment variables," and select "Edit the system environment variables".
    • Click "Environment Variables" and under "System Variables," add the following:
      • LOGGING_DIR = C:\logging
      • LOGGING_ENABLED_ASSERT = 1
      • LOGGING_ENABLED_RUNTIME = 100
  3. Restart Application: Restart Crystal Reports or your application for the changes to take effect.
  4. Run Report: Open and refresh your report. Files named crw32_[PID]_[Date]_runtime.log will appear in C:\logging. 
How to Disable Logging:
Remove the environment variables created above or rename the crlogger.dll file, ensuring you restart the application afterward. 
Locating crlogger.dll:
  • 32-bit: C:\Program Files (x86)\SAP BusinessObjects\Crystal Reports for .NET Framework 4.0\Common\SAP BusinessObjects Enterprise XI 4.0\win32_x86
  • 64-bit: C:\Program Files (x86)\SAP BusinessObjects\Crystal Reports for .NET Framework 4.0\Common\SAP BusinessObjects Enterprise XI 4.0\win32_x64 

You could also enable ODBC Trace to see what it is doing.

entry.title: Re: How to use BTP Destination with REST API in UI5 App (...
entry.content:
Hi @MioYasutake ,

thank you for answering. You are right; the credentials were invalid. I have a new one now. The correct URL is: 

url: "/backend/application/api/v1/documenthub/$metadata"

because I have defined a path for my destination as '/backend' in the 'ui5.yaml' file.

However, when I launch my app locally (in BAS) now, I get a 404 error with the new credentials.

I have also deployed my app in Cloud Foundry with the same destination, and launching it there results in an error 401 (Unauthorised). I tested this API with the same credentials and authentication method in Postman, and the response was 200.

This is my mta.yaml file

_schema-version: "3.2"
ID: testproject1
description: testproject1 with standalone approuter
version: 0.0.1

parameters:
  deploy_mode: html5-repo
  enable-parallel-deployments: true

modules:
  - name: testproject1
    type: html5
    path: .
    build-parameters:
      build-result: dist
      builder: custom
      commands:
        - npm install
        - npm run build:cf
      supported-platforms: []

  - name: testproject1-app-content
    type: com.sap.application.content
    path: .
    requires:
      - name: testproject1-repo-host
        parameters:
          content-target: true
    build-parameters:
      build-result: resources
      requires:
        - name: testproject1
          artifacts:
            - testproject1.zip
          target-path: resources/

  - name: testproject1-destination-content
    type: com.sap.application.content
    requires:
      - name: testproject1-destination-service
        parameters:
          content-target: true
      - name: testproject1-repo-host
        parameters:
          service-key:
            name: testproject1-repo-host-key
      - name: testproject1-uaa
        parameters:
          service-key:
            name: testproject1-uaa-key
    parameters:
      content:
        instance:
          existing_destinations_policy: update
          destinations:
            - Name: testproject1_html_repo_host
              ServiceInstanceName: testproject1-html5-service
              ServiceKeyName: testproject1-repo-host-key
              sap.cloud.service: testproject1

            - Name: testproject1_uaa
              Authentication: OAuth2UserTokenExchange
              ServiceInstanceName: testproject1-xsuaa-service
              ServiceKeyName: testproject1-uaa-key
              sap.cloud.service: testproject1
              
    build-parameters:
      no-source: true

  - name: testproject1-approuter
    type: approuter.nodejs
    path: approuter
    requires:
      - name: testproject1-destination-service
      - name: testproject1-uaa
      - name: testproject1-repo-runtime
    parameters:
      memory: 256M
      disk-quota: 256M
      keep-existing-routes: true

resources:
  - name: testproject1-destination-service
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    parameters:
      service: destination
      service-name: testproject1-destination-service
      service-plan: lite
      config:
        HTML5Runtime_enabled: true
        version: 1.0.0
        init_data:
          instance:
            existing_destinations_policy: update
            destinations:
              - Name: ui5
                Authentication: NoAuthentication
                ProxyType: Internet
                Type: HTTP
                URL: https://ui5.sap.com

  - name: testproject1-uaa
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    parameters:
      service: xsuaa
      service-name: testproject1-xsuaa-service
      service-plan: application
      path: ./xs-security.json

  - name: testproject1-repo-host
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    parameters:
      service: html5-apps-repo
      service-name: testproject1-html5-service
      service-plan: app-host

  - name: testproject1-repo-runtime
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    parameters:
      service: html5-apps-repo
      service-name: testproject1-html5-runtime-service
      service-plan: app-runtime

Also I have created standalone app-router for deployment:

{
  "welcomeFile": "/testproject1/index.html",
  "authenticationMethod": "route",
  "routes": [
    {
      "source": "^/backend/(.*)$",
      "target": "/$1",
      "destination": "TEST_BACKEND",
      "authenticationType": "none",
      "csrfProtection": false
    },
    {
      "source": "^/resources/(.*)$",
      "target": "/resources/$1",
      "destination": "ui5",
      "authenticationType": "none"
    },
    {
      "source": "^/test-resources/(.*)$",
      "target": "/test-resources/$1",
      "destination": "ui5",
      "authenticationType": "none"
    },
    {
      "source": "^/(.*)$",
      "target": "/$1",
      "service": "html5-apps-repo-rt",
      "authenticationType": "xsuaa"
    }
  ]
}

In Postman, I send client credentials in the body. Maybe the BTP destination processes them differently and I need to set some properties for that?

My Current Destination properties:

entry.title: Re: Business AI Makes the Data Layer More Important, Not Less
entry.content:
@appel_solar_dk 

This is a fair point. As you put it, most companies don’t choose SAP BDC or Datasphere in isolation, they choose it because SAP already runs their core business processes. SAP doesn’t really position BDC as a generic data and AI platform for non‑SAP shops, but as a way to make SAP data usable for analytics and AI without losing business context.

At the same time, SAP’s stance on openness has clearly evolved. Customers are no longer asked to “choose SAP or nothing else.” With BDC, SAP data can now be consumed in existing 3rd-party data cloud platforms like Databricks, Snowflake, BigQuery, or Fabric, using open standards such as Delta Sharing, without copying data or rebuilding rich business semantics. That’s a big shift from how SAP data was accessed in the past. Long gone are the days when SAP NetWeaver OpenHub was required to consume BW data.😉

SAP is still very deliberate about how ERP data should be accessed, but the intent is to ensure security, governance, and consistent business meaning, especially when that data drives critical financial or operational decisions. It isn’t about blocking analytics or AI innovation.

The interesting question isn’t whether SAP should open everything, but where the right balance is between openness and responsibility for mission‑critical business data.

Curious how others here see it: where should that balance sit in practice?

entry.title: Check on partner roles in PO
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

In our PO partner scheme we use the following roles:

  • OA Ordering address
  • VN Vendor
  • PI Invoicing Party
  • CA Contract address
  • GS Goods supplier

The vendor role is fixed but the others can be changed during creation or maintenance of the PO.

Business requirement is as followed: If the invoicing party is changed, it can only be changed to an invoicing party that is maintained on the BP masterdata.

So lets say I have BP 123, and on the purchasing view of the vendor role I have specified that the invoicing parties are BP 123 and BP 456. On the PO it should then be allowed to use either BP 123 or BP 456 as the invoicing party but if I try to use any other BP as the invoicing party I should get an error.

Is there a way to configure this in SAP?

Thanks & Greetings

Peter

entry.title: Virtual Landscape Stages for Cloud Integration Pipeline
entry.content:
We are excited to announce the release of version 1.0.4 of the Cloud Integration Pipeline package. This update introduces several key innovations, including the support for Virtual Backend Landscape Stages managed within a single Pipeline package and a single Integration Suite tenant.

Introduction & Motivation

This feature evolves the landscape support first introduced in version 1.0.9 of our legacy (now deprecated) all-in-one Process Integration Pipeline package. Previously, the pipeline allowed you to run scenarios across different stages without altering Partner Directory configurations - such as XSLTs for receiver determination. It achieved this by mapping the actual Sender Business System name to a "Sender Alias" based on the tenant's runtime environment.

The Challenge: The 1:1 Limitation

While the previous model worked seamlessly for 1:1 landscapes (e.g., one DEV tenant for one DEV backend), it struggled with multiple backend landscapes in one tenant. A common challenge arises when a single non-production SAP Integration Suite tenant is used to integrate both Development and Quality Assurance backends (e.g., S/4HANA).

In such cases, the landscape stage was "locked" to the tenant. You could define the stage for S/4HANA DEV or S/4HANA QA, but not both simultaneously. This forced integrators into a workaround: one system would have to be defined with its actual technical name (e.g., S4DCLNT100) in the Partner Directory, breaking the clean abstraction that the pipeline aims to provide.

The Solution: Virtual Stages

The new Virtual Stages feature is designed specifically for non-productive tenants where a single integration scenario must serve multiple landscape stages of the same backend.

Historically, achieving this in SAP Process Orchestration (PO) was also cumbersome. Typical approaches:

  • Backend Inbound Scenarios: Creating "dummy" XPath conditions in Receiver Determinations to switch between receivers.
  • Backend Outbound Scenarios: Duplicating Integrated Configurations (ICOs) for every actual sender.

The Cloud Integration Pipeline now provides an elegant, single-package solution for these requirements. In the next increments it is planned to add an option to define Integration Scenario in the context of a stage (e.g. if Scenario changed in Development stage but still old version is used in Quality).

Core Use Cases

We have optimized this framework to cover three primary architectural scenarios:

  1. Consolidated Non-Prod Environments: Supporting a 3-tier backend landscape using a 2-tier Integration Suite setup. This allows you to manage multiple backend stages through one pipeline in a single tenant.
  2. Dual-Track Backend Support: SAP S/4HANA landscapes often utilize "Maintenance" and "Project" tracks. Maintaining a dual-track middleware landscape to match this is a significant overhead. Virtual Stages allow you to support track-based routing for the backend using a single middleware instance.
  3. Cross-Stage Multicasting: More exotic use case - you may need to route a single message from a sender to multiple stages of the same receiver (e.g., sending data to both MDG-DEV and MDG-QA simultaneously). Since the Receiver Alias (MDG) remains constant, the pipeline now supports multicasting across virtual stages without requiring extra routing branches or modifications to Receiver Determination rules (they can be kept the same as in Production tenant).

Leveraging Authenticated Users for Dynamic Inbound Processing

Integration scenarios triggered from external sources (push-based) carry the SapAuthenticatedUserName header (available if it is added to the IFlow’s "Allowed Headers" list). This header contains the Client ID used by the caller. In SAP Integration Suite, a unique Client ID is generated for every Service Instance created via the BTP Cockpit.

Historically, the Pipeline determined the Scenario ID using the SAP_Sender (system name) and SAP_SenderInterface (interface name) headers. While effective, this model makes it difficult to build truly generic inbound processing steps for certain adapters.

The Challenge with Generic Inbound Adapters

Determining a sender interface dynamically is relatively straightforward (e.g., using XPath on an XML root or parsing the URI path in an HTTPS adapter). However, identifying the Sender System is much harder when that information isn't part of the payload or provided by standard adapter headers.

A prime example is the SOAP/SAP RM adapter. It lacks standard headers to identify the sender system and does not provide a unique URI path for the called service. Without a static header, dynamic sender determination becomes a technical hurdle.

The Solution: Identity-Based Determination

By supporting the SapAuthenticatedUserName header, the Pipeline can now implement Identity-Based Sender Determination. Instead of relying on manually populated headers (e.g. from externalized parameters), the Pipeline resolves the sender by mapping the caller's Client ID to a specific system or context in the Partner Directory.

This not only simplifies generic IFlow design but also provides a foundation for the Virtual Stages functionality. Two primary configuration patterns are now supported:

  1. Landscape Stage Resolution: Map an Authenticated User to a Partner ID using the Landscape_Stage~ prefix in the Partner Directory. This allows you to automatically identify the landscape stage (e.g., DEV, QA) across all scenarios based on which service key the sender uses.
  2. System-Specific Mapping: Map an Authenticated User directly to a Business System Partner ID. This identifies the exact business system via the caller's identity, effectively replacing the need for a static SAP_Sender header across multiple sender systems.

Architecture Overview

A single Integration Suite tenant can now host multiple parallel "stages" for your business systems (e.g., QA~Project, QA~Maintenance, Pre-Production, etc.).

To map your tenant to these Landscape Stages (also known as Virtual Stages), you can choose between two approaches:

  • Structured Assignment: Explicitly assign all possible virtual stages to the Partner SAP_Integration_Suite_Landscape in the Partner Directory.
  • Dynamic Promotion: If a stage is supplied at runtime - via a header or the Authenticated User approach - that differs from the global configuration, the pipeline "promotes" this value into the current context.

The only prerequisite is that these Virtual Stages must be maintained in the Partner Directory for the respective Sender or Receiver System Aliases.

Core Logic: Identity & Scenario Resolution

The primary goal of the pipeline is to determine the Partner ID of an Integration Scenario. Traditionally, this relies on the SAP_Sender (Sender System name that is checked for System Alias existence) and SAP_SenderInterface headers. We have extended this logic to resolve the Virtual Stage and Sender Context before identifying the Scenario ID.

After running this resolution logic, the landscape stage could remain ambiguous (exact stage is unknown) - in this case multiple possible stages are transferred in the header tenantStage and the pipeline will try to navigate the required routing logic in the Receiver Stage determination.

The virtual stage routing follows a specific guardrail: the logic only executes if the header tenantStageVirtual is set to 'true'. This ensures that virtualization overhead never affects Production tenants. During Receiver Stage discovery, the system evaluates the target landscape. If the routing rules identify multiple valid virtual stages for a single Receiver Alias (only possible for Asynchronous processing), a Message Split occurs. The source message is then multicasted to all determined stages (e.g., both a Maintenance and a Project track). If only a single stage is identified, the tenantStage header is updated, and the message proceeds without multicasting.

Receiver Stage Discovery

The discovery process depends heavily on whether the Tenant Stage is already known and whether the Receiver is defined via a System Alias (abstraction) rather than a physical Business System.

Receiver Landscape Stage makes only sense for Receivers with maintained System Alias:

Scenario 1: The Tenant Stage is Known

When the Stage is identified, the system follows the 5-Step Routing Hierarchy:

  • Stage-Specific Rule Override: Use a specific rule to redirect the "Natural Choice" to a different stage. Stage-Specific Rule can be defined at the Configuration Scenario or at Receiver Alias level.
  • Natural Choice: By default, route to the identical stage of the receiver (e.g., DEV to DEV).
  • Tenant-Level Fallback Rules: A Tenant-Wide rule maintained at the Configuration Scenario or at Receiver Alias level, independent of the specific stage.

Scenario 2: The Tenant Stage is Unknown (Ambiguous)

If the Stage is unknown, the "Natural Choice" is unavailable. The system then evaluates:

  • Tenant-Level Rules: A Tenant-Wide rule maintained at the Configuration Scenario or at Receiver Alias level, independent of the specific stage.
  • Deterministic Exit: If the Receiver Alias has only one possible stage, the system auto-routes to that single destination.

To implement these rules, you must maintain String parameters in the Partner Directory using the following naming convention:

  • Parameter ID: stageRule_ + {Stage Name OR Tenant Name} + optional ~{Receiver Alias}.
  • Value Syntax: The character > followed by a comma-separated list of target stages.

Examples:

  • Stage-Specific with Receiver Alias: stageRule_DEV-MNT~S4HANA with value >DEV,QA-PRJ
  • Tenant-Wide without Receiver Alias: stageRule_My-IS-Tenant with value >QA-MNT

For a deep dive into the syntax and advanced configuration options, please refer to the Landscape Stages.

Sample Scenarios

In the following, we show you how to setup flexible stages and stage rules based on a couple of sample scenarios. Much more options are supported than shown here. For more details about all the options, see Landscape Stages.

As a prerequisite, we first need to create a partner ID SAP_Integration_Suite_Landscape in the Partner Directory where we maintain the list off all virtual stages as shown below. As you can see, the DEV tenant is mapped to two stages whereas the QA tenant is mapped to 3 stages. For PRD, we do not have virtual stages, here we have a 1:1 mapping.

Scenario passing message header to pipeline

In the first scenario, we simply pass the virtual stage via the message header tenantStage from the scenario-specific inbound processing flow to the generic flows of the pipeline. Furthermore, we have defined the sender system name, the sender interface name as well as the sender interface namespace in the scenario-specific inbound processing flow which is used to determine the configuration scenario. As you can see, we have externalized all parameters so that you can configure the parameters without need of changing the flow.

Because the stage name is passed via the message header, no other check is carried out to determine the stage. The pipeline then maps the sender business system, here BS_05E_S1QTST, and the corresponding stage, here QA-TST, to the sender alias, here BC_05E_S1. As you can see, we apply the following naming convention for the Partner Directory entries: We use the prefix BS (Business System) for the business system name and BC (Business Component) for the alias. Btw, for the configuration scenarios we use the prefix CS, see below.

Using the sender alias, we now determine the configuration scenario. For this, a corresponding alternative partner needs to be maintained in the Partner Directory.

Switching to the String Parameters, you notice that our example is a Point-to-Point scenario where we defined the receiver, here alias BC_05E_R1, and the end point of the scenario-specific outbound processing flow as string parameters. Furthermore, you can see that we have defined a stage rule for the stage QA-TST. In our case, when sending a message from the stage QA-TST, it should be duplicated and sent out to stages QA-PRJ and QA-TST.

Now that we have determined the receiver alias, we map the alias to the actual business systems via the corresponding Partner Directory entry.

When exchanging a message, the message processing log looks as follows.

Scenario using authorized user

In the second scenario, we use authorized users to determine the stage. Here, we have two options: either map an authorized user  to a partner ID with prefix Landscape_Stage or to a business system.

The alternative partner of this configuration scenario looks as follows. Here, we map the alias BC_05D_S1 to the configuration scenario.

Option 1

For this option, we pass sender system name (here BS_05D_S1DMNT), sender interface name and sender interface namespace to the pipeline. Furthermore, we use a particular service key to authenticate to the Cloud Integration runtime. This service key should only be used for systems of the stage DEV-MNT. We now map the client ID of the service key as authorized user to a partner ID with prefix Landscape_Stage, in this case Landscape_Stage~DEV-MNT. This way, we are able to directly determine the stage by fetching the prefix of the partner ID.

Furthermore, the business system BS_05D_S1DMNT is mapped to the alias BC_05D_S1 which is used in the configuration scenario above.

Like in the example before, we define a stage rule, this time for a specific receiver alias, here BC_05D_R1, and the stage DEV-MNT.

When exchanging a message, the message processing log looks as follows. The client ID is used to determine the stage. Using this very stage and the business system name, the system alias is determined and with this the configuration scenario and the receiver. The stage rule is applied and hence the message is sent to two receiving business systems.

Option 2

For this option, we do not pass any sender system name to the pipeline, instead we determine the sender business system and the virtual stage by matching an authorized user to a business system in the Partner Directory. Note, here we match the client ID of a dedicated service key to a business system name and not to an alias, in our case BS_05D_S1DPRJ.

On the String Parameters tab of the partner ID BS_05D_S1DPRJ, we have defined the stage DEV-PRJ as value of the ID landscapeStage. When authenticating to the Cloud Integration runtime using this very service key user, both are determined the business system name and the virtual stage, latter via the string parameter.

Furthermore, the business system BS_05D_S1DPRJ is mapped to the alias BC_05D_S1 which is used in the configuration scenario above.

This time, we haven't defined any stage rule. So, the message processing log then looks as follows.

 

Conclusion and Outlook

 

The concept of Virtual Stages has been a consistent objective since the initial introduction of Landscape Stages. Finding the balance between robust functionality, feature clarity, and configuration simplicity was the primary architectural challenge during development. Because these changes impact the core pipeline routing engine, our priority was ensuring stability and avoiding regressions while incorporating these new configuration options.

It is important to note a current limitation: Virtual Stage Routing currently requires that a single tenant share the same Configuration Scenario across all its stages.

In practice, there is often a need for version divergence. For example, consider an existing point-to-point interface. If a change request requires adding a new receiver, you might want to transition that interface to use Receiver Determination in the Development stage while maintaining the original Point-to-Point logic in the Quality Assurance stage.

Currently, this "cross-version" setup is not yet supported because Scenario ID determination relies exclusively on the Sender and the Sender Interface. The Stage Context is not yet a factor in identifying the Configuration Scenario.

However, this is the intended direction for the framework. Integrating stage-awareness into the scenario determination logic is a planned update for upcoming increments. For now, this version provides a functional foundation for any landscape where the Configuration Scenario remains consistent across all tracks.

entry.title: Enhanced IDoc inbound support for Cloud Integration Pipeline
entry.content:
We are excited to announce the release of version 1.0.7 of the Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Inbound package with major enhancements of the Pipeline v2 Generic Step01 - Inbound Processing for Idoc flow. This enhancements include support for partner type not equal LS and support for so-called virtual receiver in order to match the incoming IDoc message to a configuration scenario partner ID in the Partner Directory.

In order to determine the partner ID of the configuration scenario, the generic IDoc inbound processing flow checks if an alternative partner exists that matches the sender information, the sender interface name and eventually the sender interface namespace derived from the IDoc control header of the incoming IDoc message.

The sender interface name is a combination of the message type MESTYP, the IDoc type IDOCTYP, and optionally, if not empty, the extension type CIMTYP. The namespace can be taken into account or not. In our examples below, we use it.

The agency of the alternative partner is derived from the sender information of the IDoc control header. Its definition however depends on the partner type, i.e., whether it's a logical system (LS) or not (like LI, KU, etc.), and whether landscape stages (e.g., DEV, QA, PRD or similar) are used or not. See Landscape Stages. Furthermore, you have the option to use a so-called virtual receiver. In this case, both the sender partner information and the receiver partner information provided in the IDoc control record is considered to determine the partner ID of the configuration scenario.

Btw, in order to include the check for the virtual receiver, you need to configure the externalized parameter CustomXPid_ResolveVirtualReceiver of the generic IDoc inbound processing flow to true. By default, it's false hence disabled to reduce the number of overall checks assuming that the usage of a virtual receiver is not frequently used. If the virtual receiver is enabled, we first check if we can find a match including the receiver information. In case that no match is found, we then check if only the sender matches. This rule is especially discussed in the very last sample scenario below.

For a detailed description of all configuration options, see Determine Partner ID for IDoc Inbound.

In the following, I will go through some sample configurations to better understand the new features and configuration options when it comes to IDoc inbound scenarios. For most of the scenarios we keep it simple and just have configured the Point-to-Point pattern.

Furthermore, we stick to the definition of the Agency in the alternative partner. For all scenarios the Scheme is defined as combination of message type FLIGHTBOOKING_CREATEFROMDAT and IDoc type FLIGHTBOOKING_CREATEFROMDAT01, and the ID equals urn:sap-com:document:sap:idoc:messages.

Incoming IDoc with sender partner type = LS, no usage of landscape stages

In this sample scenario, we do not use landscape stage and the sender partner type SNDPRT of the incoming IDoc message equals LS. In this case, the sender partner number SNDPRN equals the logical system name of the sender. So, on the tab Alternative Partners of the configuration scenario we define the Agency equals the partner number SNDPRN, in our case O6DCLNT607.

As said, in most cases we simply configure a Point-to-Point scenario. So, on the String Parameters tab we define the receiver and the end point of the scenario-specific outbound processing flow as follows:

When exchanging an IDoc using the pipeline for Cloud Integration, the message processing log of the generic IDoc inbound processing flow has been enhanced to provide further details about the IDoc sender partner, the partner ID of the configuration scenario determined and the alternative partner which at the end matches to the partner ID. In this case, we send an IDoc of sender partner type LS and sender partner number O6DCLNT607. So, you can see from the Custom Headers section of the log that the partner number O6DCLNT607 led to the configuration scenario CS_PIP_Samples_Scenario_06D. The IDoc sender partner is defined as combination of the sender partner type, the sender partner number and the sender port, here LS.O6DCLNT607.SAPO6D.

Sender partner type = LS, virtual receiver ≠ LS, without landscape stages

In the next sample scenario, like in the example before we do not use landscape stage and the sender partner type SNDPRT of the incoming IDoc message equals LS. This time however, we use a virtual receiver not equal LS. The Agency is then defined as a combination of the logical system name of the sender and a representation of the receiver party separated by a colon ":".

The receiver party follows the pattern ALE#<RCVPRT>[#<RCVPFC>]#<RCVPRN> with <RCVPRT> the value of the receiver partner type, <RCVPFC> the value of the receiver partner function, and <RCVPRN> the receiver partner number.

In our example, the sender partner number SNDPRN equals J6QCLNT800. In the sending business system, the receiver partner is defined as sold-to-party customer (partner type KU and partner function SP) with customer number 9934567890. The Agency is then defined as follows: J6QCLNT800:ALE#KU#SP#9934567890.

When exchanging an IDoc fitting to the configuration scenario, the message processing log contains all relevant details like seen above. This time, you also get the IDoc receiver partner displayed as combination of the receiver partner type, the receiver partner function, the receiver partner number and the receiver port.

Sender partner type ≠ LS, virtual receiver ≠ LS, with landscape stages

Now, let's look at a configuration using landscape stage. When using landscape stages, we usually map the actual business system name to a system alias. Actually, because we exchange IDoc messages here, we rather map the logical system name to the alias. To determine the partner ID and the receivers we only use alias names within the pipeline. This way, the configuration scenario can be defined independent of the landscape stage, e.g., the XSLT containing the XPath conditions to determining the receivers and interfaces contains alias only and hence doesn't have to be modified when being used across the different tenants. See Landscape Stages.

In this sample scenario, we again use virtual receiver. Both, the sender partner type and the receiver partner type are not equal LS.

Assuming, we test the scenario in the QA landscape. The sender business system in QA and the sender partner are defined as follows:

  • Sender business system name equals BS_06K_S1Q.
  • Sender partner type SNDPRT equals KU.
  • Sender partner number SNDPRN equals 0000112015.
  • Sender port SNDPOR equals SAPK6Q.
  • System client MANDT equals 400.

In the sender business system, the receiver partner is defined as follows:

  • Receiver partner type RCVPRT equals LI.
  • Receiver partner number RCVPRN equals 0000012935.
  • Receiver port RCVPOR equals A000000012.

Because the sender partner type is not a logical system name, we first need to derive the logical system name of the sender from the sender port and the client. We only support sender ports that follow the pattern SAP<SID> with <SID> the system ID, here K6Q. This results into the logical system name K6QCLNT400 which is then mapped to a partner ID representing the alias of the sender system, here BC_06K_S1. You may have noticed that we apply the following naming convention for the Partner Directory entries: We use the prefix BS (Business System) for the business system name and BC (Business Component) for the alias. For the configuration scenarios we actually use the prefix CS.

For partners of type not equal LS, we can map the IDoc control record fields that describe the partner to a party alias. Usually, you choose this option when using landscape stages. So, for the sender we create a partner in the Partner Directory representing the sender party, here PRTY_06K_Blue. The alternative partner to map to this party is then defined as follows:

  • Agency: Name of the sender alias, here BC_06K_S1.
  • Scheme: ALE#<SNDPRT>[#<SNDPFC>], here ALE#KU.
  • ID: <SNDPRN>, here 0000112015.

The same applies for the receiver party. Here, we create a new partner PRTY_06K_Amber in the Partner Directory with the following alternative partner:

  • Agency: Name of the sender alias, here BC_06K_S1.
  • Scheme: ALE#<RCVPRT>[#<RCVPFC>], here ALE#LI.
  • ID: <RCVPRN>, here 0000012935.

Now let's take a look at the alternative partner to match the sender and the virtual receiver to the configuration scenario. Like in the example before, the Agency is then defined as a combination of a sender part and a receiver part separated by a colon ":".

For the sender part, we apply the same pattern like we do for party support in the pipeline in general, i.e., we combine the party alias and the system alias separated by a period, ".", in our case PRTY_06K_Blue.BS_06K_S1.

For the receiver part, we stick to the party alias only, here PRTY_06K_Amber.

This results into the Agency PRTY_06K_Blue.BS_06K_S1:PRTY_06K_Amber.

When exchanging an IDoc fitting to the configuration scenario, the message processing log contains all relevant details like seen above.

Sender partner type = LS, virtual receiver ≠ LS, with fallback configuration, with landscape stages

Our last example is the most complex one. This example actually comprises multiple configuration scenarios. We send out IDoc messages from a business system to multiple partners. The sender partner type equals LS whereas the receiver partner type for all receiver partners is KU with partner function AG. For some partners, individual configuration scenarios exist using virtual receiver. Those are of pattern Point-to-Point. Where such a match is not found, we then check for a match without virtual receiver and have defined a sort of fallback configuration scenario. The fallback configuration scenario is of pattern Content-Based-Router where an XSLT mapping is carried out to determine the receiver.

We use landscape stages, so we first map the logical system name of the sender M6QCLNT107 to a partner ID representing the alias of the sender system, here BC_06M_S1.

Furthermore, we map the partner numbers of the receiver partners to party aliases in the Partner Directory, here partner number 0000077891 with scheme ALE#KU#AG is mapped to party alias PRTY_06M_White.

Similarly for PRTY_06M_Grey. Here partner number 0000077892 with scheme ALE#KU#AG is mapped to party alias PRTY_06M_Grey.

In the configuration scenario for the receiver-specific configuration scenario for the party PRTY_06M_White, the Agency of the alternative partner is defined as a combination of the sender alias and the receiver party alias: BC_06M_S1.PRTY_06M_White.

As said, the receiver-specific configuration scenarios are of pattern Point-to-Point where we define receiver and end point of the receiver-specific outbound processing flow as string parameters.

The configuration scenario for the receiver-specific configuration scenario for the party PRTY_06M_Grey is defined in a similar way. The Agency of the alternative partner is defined as follows: BC_06M_S1.PRTY_06M_Grey.

The fallback configuration scenario kicks in if a potential virtual receiver won't match to a receiver-specific configuration scenario. In this case, in a second attempt, the IDoc inbound processing flow checks for a match using the sender alias nonly. Hence the Agency of the fallback configuration scenario is defined as sender alias BC_06M_S1.

On the Binary Parameters tab, an XSLT needs to be uploaded with ID receiverDetermination which is carried out to determine the receiver.

The XSLT looks as follows. The XPaths actually depend on the receiver partner numbers from the IDoc control record of the incoming messages.

So, let's now send an IDoc message for the receiver with customer number 0000077891 to the pipeline. As you can see from the message processing log, the receiver-specific configuration scenario for the party PRTY_06M_White has been determined.

When we send an IDoc message for the receiver with customer number 0000077893 to the pipeline, the fallback configuration scenario has been determined.

Hope those sample scenarios help you to better understand the configuration options for IDoc inbound scenarios using the new features in the pipeline for Cloud Integration.

As we have used landscape stages in some of the sample scenarios, you may be curious about the latest enhancements that we have shipped to improve the support for landscape stages. See Virtual Landscape Stages for Cloud Integration Pipeline.

entry.title: Re: Clarification on Maintain WBS Bill of Material App Us...
entry.content:
Please find below answers to your questions

1) Purpose of the Maintain WBS BOM App
It allows you to define a Bill of Material directly at the WBS element level. Unlike a material BOM (which is global and reusable), a WBS BOM is project‑specific and tied to the WBS hierarchy. It is intended for industries where every project has unique material requirements, so you don’t want to maintain hundreds of separate material BOMs.

2) How EPPC / Project‑Based Companies use it
For each project WBS, planners list the required materials and quantities in the WBS BOM. This acts as a project‑specific material list for procurement and logistics. It is especially useful in construction, engineering, and EPC projects where no two projects have identical material needs.

3) Link to Procurement (PR/PO)
Once the WBS BOM is created, you can run MRP for the project/WBS. MRP will read the WBS BOM, generate planned requirements, and create purchase requisitions. Those PRs flow into the procurement process (PO creation, supplier collaboration). The linkage is automatic. The WBS BOM drives the demand, and procurement documents are tied back to the WBS element for cost tracking.

4) Different Materials and Quantities per Project
Yes — each WBS BOM is independent. You can maintain different materials, quantities, and even alternative items for each project WBS. This flexibility is the main advantage over standard material BOMs.

5) Recommended Approach
For project‑based procurement where materials vary per project, the WBS BOM is the recommended approach in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. It keeps procurement aligned with project structures, ensures costs are collected at the right WBS, and avoids the overhead of maintaining countless material BOMs. It also integrates seamlessly with project MRP and procurement apps.

In short:

Purpose: Project‑specific BOM tied to WBS.
Usage: Defines material needs per project.
Procurement link: Drives PR/PO creation via MRP.
Flexibility: Different materials/quantities per WBS.
Best practice: Yes, this is the recommended approach for EPC/EPPC scenarios.

entry.title: New features in the Pipeline for Cloud Integration packages with the March 2026 shipment
entry.content:
We have just shipped new versions of the Pipeline for Cloud Integration packages. This time we implemented two major features, namely support for more flexible landscape stages and major changes in the generic IDoc inbound processing flow. The following new versions have been shipped:

  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Inbound version 1.0.7
  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Pipelines version 1.0.4
  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Outbound version 1.0.3
  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Templates version 1.1.1

The following new features have been shipped at a glance:

  • Supporting IDocs with partner type other than LS such as LI or KU. Before, we only supported partner type LS. Furthermore, you have the option to include a so called virtual receiver to determine the partner ID similar to how we support IDoc inbound scenarios in SAP Process Orchestration. For more details, see Determine Partner ID for IDoc Inbound. Furthermore, check out this blog post Enhanced IDoc inbound support for Cloud Integration Pipeline.
  • Support for more flexible landscape stages taking into account different landscape stages of your business systems compared to the number of SAP Integration Suite tenants. E.g., having only two tenants one for dev and qa and the other for prod. So, you have the option to define multiple stages per tenant and to define so called stage rules in case that you like to route a message from one stage to another. Furthermore, different options to determine the stage are supported: via authorized user, via header passed to the pipeline steps or via configuration parameter in the Partner Directory. For more details, see Landscape Stages. Furthermore, check out this blog post Virtual Landscape Stages for Cloud Integration Pipeline.
  • For the generic XI outbound flow, we now support dynamic headers like we already do for the generic XI inbound. See Dynamic Headers chapter in XI Sender Adapter and XI Receiver Adapter.
  • For the generic XI outbound flow, you can now override the headers SAP_Sender and SAP_Receiver from the default externalized parameters. If the scenario-specific inbound processing flow passes empty header values to the generic XI outbound flow, the values of the externalized parameters are used. See very last note at XI Receiver Adapter.
  • The generic IDoc outbound flow has been enhanced to display the partner functions SNDPFC and RCVPFC in the custom header properties section of the message processing log in message monitoring in addition to partner type, partner port and partner number.
  • The generic IDoc outbound flow now fails if any mandatory control record field is empty or missing. Before, this was ignored which however would have led to an error in the backend systems.
  • We fixed an issue with a wrong type in a content modifier of the template Pipeline v2 Template Step01 - Inbound Processing Synchronous in the templates package, it was Constant before, now the correct type is Expression.

Please update the packages in your Design area to benefit from the changes. Though we run extensive regression tests and new feature tests before publishing a new version, we nevertheless recommend to update your dev and test landscape first and to test there before updating the content on your prod tenant.

entry.title: Demo: Conversational Analytics with SAP Analytics Cloud in Business Data Cloud
entry.content:
Today’s business moves fast. And to keep up, we need to make decisions quickly and with confidence. But even in data-rich organizations, that’s not always easy, too often the insights people need are locked behind tools, dashboards, or technical expertise. Despite heavy investment in analytics, many business analysts and decision-makers still struggle to access the right data at the right time. They may know what they need, but not where to find it, or how to get it fast enough.

That’s the problem conversational analytics solve, thanks to generative AI, business analysts and all and decision-makers can now ask questions in plain language. Like “Show me actuals versus forecast by region.” And instantly, they get clear, contextual insights, no delays.

Watch a demo of conversational analytics with SAC in BDC

In this video, first, we demonstrate the just ask feature for natural language queries. With the just ask NLQ feature in SAP Analytics Cloud, business analysts can ask questions in plain language.
Then we demonstrate analytical insights in the Joule AI copilot. Joule takes the NLQ experience to next level, with a real conversational analytics experience for all business users, right where they work, no switching tools.
The result is faster decisions, higher productivity, and a culture where using data becomes effortless and automatic.

Recommended Resources

Blog Post: Conversational analytics: Why rich metadata and business semantics matter more than ever

Blog Post: SAP Business Data Cloud for the Business Analyst: turning raw data to trusted and actionable insight

Hands-on: SAP Business Data Cloud basic trial

entry.title: Re: Showing Custom fields in QM Notification Item Overvie...
entry.content:
Dear @inamullah,

In Public Cloud there is no SAP-documented standard extension point to add an editable custom input field directly in the QM Notification Items Overview table for inline maintenance. SAP supported extensibility for this scenario is at item detail level for the business context Notification Item/Defect the custom field is maintained via Items tab->select item->Detail View (dialog box), which is the UI location documented by SAP for this extension. SAP also documents that custom fields can be displayed in quality notification worklist apps, including Change Quality Notification Items Worklist. However this refers to display in worklists, not to adding an inline editable field in the Items Overview table inside the notification screen

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d1e58be39d884a0dbf75a7526a9acbf4/b392cc6291ea442a8c7636f05284dd24.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d1e58be39d884a0dbf75a7526a9acbf4/c2449b9951d6448aab185c7cebd38539.html

 

Summary:
The supported standard approach is to create the custom field for Notification Item/Defect and maintain it in Item Detail View. A direct inline input field in the QM Notification Items Overview table is not covered by SAP documented standard extensibility for Public Cloud

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Unable to import AP Credit Memo with Withholding tax...
entry.content:
Hi @satinath_biswas ,

The values in the following columns are not required in your scenario: WTCode, WTAmount, and TaxableAmount.

If WTCode is set, the information from the PCH5 table of the A/P Invoices document will not be copied to the RPC5 table of the A/P Credit Memos. Additionally, when TaxableAmount and WTAmount are set, the withholding amount information for other currencies and TDS-related details will not be populated.

Therefore, please set values only for the BaseDocEntry and BaseDocType columns in the WithholdingTaxData object. The import should then proceed successfully.

Kind regards,

ANKIT CHAUHAN

SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Is it possible to connect two XI adapters to the same...
